questionText: Before marrying Dana, Lee, a member of a fraternal insurance association, orally told Dana that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana married Lee. No written agreement memorializing that promise was ever signed by Lee. After the marriage, Lee filed the association’s standard form naming Dana as beneficiary. The association’s by-laws allowed a member to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and several years afterward Lee followed that procedure and named his two children from an earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries. When Lee died, Dana claimed the proceeds based on Lee’s pre-marriage promise and his earlier designation of her after the wedding. The children claimed the proceeds under the later beneficiary change. Who has the better claim to the insurance proceeds? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
The children have the better claim.

Lee’s premarital statement to Dana was an antenuptial promise made in consideration of marriage. A promise to transfer property, or to provide insurance benefits, in return for marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, no signed writing memorialized any agreement by Lee that Dana would remain the beneficiary or that he was surrendering his right to change beneficiaries. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that problem; marriage is the very consideration that brings the promise within the statute, so it is not the kind of part performance that takes the agreement out of the statute.

Dana’s second argument—that Lee actually did name her beneficiary after the wedding—is also weak. In a fraternal benefit or insurance association, the member’s certificate and the association’s by-laws control. Those by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. That means Dana’s designation gave her only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right, unless Lee had separately bound himself by an enforceable contract not to change the designation. He did not.

The post-marriage beneficiary form does not change that result. It shows that Lee at one point designated Dana as beneficiary, but it does not show a contractual promise that the designation was irrevocable, nor does it satisfy the Statute of Frauds as a memorandum of an antenuptial agreement. It says nothing about marriage as consideration and nothing about Lee giving up his reserved power to redesignate beneficiaries.

Because Lee later followed the association’s prescribed procedure and validly changed the beneficiary designation to his children, the children take the proceeds. Dana cannot defeat that later designation based on the oral premarital promise, and her earlier status as named beneficiary did not vest into an enforceable right.

So, legally, the insurance proceeds should go to Lee’s children.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to Dana is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. A promise made “in consideration of marriage” generally must be in a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, Lee’s promise was: if Dana married him, he would make her the beneficiary. That is a classic marriage-consideration agreement. Because no signed writing memorialized it, Dana cannot enforce that promise as a contract against Lee’s estate or against the insurance proceeds.

Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually save the oral agreement. In this context, marriage is the consideration for the promise, and the Statute of Frauds specifically requires such agreements to be written. Courts generally do not treat the subsequent marriage as the kind of part performance that removes the agreement from the statute.

Dana’s second argument—that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the marriage—is also weak because of the nature of the interest she received. Under the association’s by-laws, Lee retained the right to change beneficiaries by following the prescribed procedure. He later did exactly that by filing the required sworn request and naming his children. In fraternal insurance arrangements of this kind, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right, so long as the member reserves the power to change the beneficiary and complies with the association’s rules in doing so.

So:

1. The oral antenuptial promise is unenforceable because it was not in writing.
2. Dana’s later designation as beneficiary did not give her a vested interest, because Lee retained and validly exercised the right to change beneficiaries.
3. The children, as the beneficiaries named in Lee’s valid later change, are entitled to the proceeds.

Unless there were additional facts showing fraud, waiver, or some independent written contract giving Dana vested rights, the proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is the weak point in her case. A promise made “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, that type of antenuptial agreement generally must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Here, there was no signed writing by Lee memorializing that bargain. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not by itself make the oral agreement enforceable; marriage is the very consideration that brings the promise within the statute.

That means Dana cannot rely on the oral engagement-period promise as an enforceable contract that froze her status as beneficiary.

Her second argument—that Lee in fact named her beneficiary after the marriage—is also not enough to defeat the children’s claim. In a fraternal insurance association, the member’s rights and the beneficiary’s rights are controlled by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Those by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and he complied with that procedure. In that setting, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, until the member dies. So Dana’s earlier designation gave her no irrevocable entitlement unless there was some separate enforceable agreement by Lee not to change the beneficiary.

But the only supposed separate agreement is the oral pre-marriage promise, and that agreement is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. The later beneficiary designation form naming Dana does not cure that problem unless it amounts to a sufficient signed memorandum of the antenuptial contract itself. On these facts, it appears to be only the association’s standard beneficiary designation, not a writing stating that Lee agreed, in consideration of marriage, to keep Dana as beneficiary permanently. So it does not convert the oral antenuptial promise into an enforceable contract.

Accordingly, because Lee validly exercised his by-law right to change beneficiaries and Dana had no vested contractual right preventing him from doing so, the later designation of the children controls. The children are entitled to the proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is likely unenforceable because it was an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage must generally be in a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, Lee’s promise was oral only. Dana’s marriage to Lee was the consideration for the promise, so this is exactly the kind of agreement the statute covers. The fact that Dana did marry him does not usually cure the lack of a signed writing; marriage alone is generally not enough part performance to remove such an agreement from the statute.

Dana’s fallback argument is that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the wedding. But that earlier designation did not give her a vested right to the proceeds, because the association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. In fraternal insurance and benefit-association cases, where the governing rules reserve to the member the right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a fixed property interest. Her rights do not vest until the member dies without making a valid change.

Lee later complied with the by-laws and validly changed the designation to his two children. Once he did that, the children became the beneficiaries entitled to the proceeds.

Dana could only defeat that result if she could show some enforceable contract that bound Lee not to change the beneficiary and gave her an equitable right to the policy proceeds. But the only such contract alleged is the oral antenuptial promise, and that is barred by the Statute of Frauds. So she cannot use that promise to override the later valid beneficiary change.

Bottom line: the later beneficiary designation controls, and the children should receive the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s strongest argument is that Lee made an enforceable premarital contract: if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his insurance. If that contract were enforceable, a court could potentially impose a constructive trust on the proceeds even though Lee later changed the named beneficiary. But the problem is that Lee’s promise was oral.

A promise made in consideration of marriage, other than the mutual promise to marry itself, falls within the Statute of Frauds. A promise such as “marry me and I will make you beneficiary of my insurance” is exactly the kind of antenuptial agreement that ordinarily must be in a signed writing by the promisor. Here, no writing memorializing that bargain was signed by Lee. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually take the agreement out of the Statute of Frauds, because the statute specifically targets agreements based on marriage consideration.

Lee’s later filing of the association’s beneficiary form naming Dana does not cure that problem. That form shows only that he designated her as beneficiary at that time. It does not adequately memorialize an enforceable antenuptial contract that he would keep her as beneficiary or that the designation was irrevocable in exchange for marriage.

That leaves Dana with her second theory: that once Lee named her beneficiary after the marriage, she became entitled to the proceeds. But under the association’s by-laws, Lee retained the right to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. In fraternal insurance arrangements of that sort, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, so long as the member reserves the power to change the beneficiary. Lee later exercised that reserved power in the manner required by the by-laws and named his children. That later change is effective unless Dana can show some separate, enforceable agreement limiting Lee’s right to change the beneficiary. Because her only such agreement is the unenforceable oral premarital promise, she cannot.

So the legal result is:

1. The oral promise to make Dana beneficiary in consideration of marriage is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.
2. Dana’s earlier designation as beneficiary gave her only a revocable expectancy, because the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries.
3. Lee validly changed the beneficiary designation to his children before death.

Therefore, the children are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is the weak point in her case. A promise made “in consideration of marriage” generally falls within the Statute of Frauds and is unenforceable unless it is in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s promise was not merely a promise to marry; it was a promise to confer a financial benefit if Dana married him. Because no signed writing memorialized that promise, Dana ordinarily cannot enforce it as a binding antenuptial contract.

Lee’s later act of naming Dana as beneficiary after the marriage does not cure that problem. That designation gave Dana whatever rights the insurance contract and the association’s by-laws gave a named beneficiary. But in a fraternal insurance association, where the by-laws expressly allow the member to change beneficiaries by following a prescribed procedure, the named beneficiary usually has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested property right. Here, the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later complied with that procedure and named his children. Once he did so, the children became the valid beneficiaries under the certificate.

Dana might argue that equity should enforce Lee’s original promise because she married him in reliance on it. But that argument usually fails where the underlying antenuptial promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds and where the insurance contract itself reserved to Lee the unrestricted right to substitute beneficiaries. Courts generally will not impose a constructive trust in favor of the former beneficiary when the insured had the contractual right to make the later change, unless there was an enforceable separate agreement limiting that right. Dana has no such enforceable agreement because the promise was oral.

So the analysis is:

1. The oral promise before marriage is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as an agreement made in consideration of marriage.
2. Dana’s post-marriage designation as beneficiary gave her only a defeasible expectancy because the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries.
3. Lee validly exercised that right and named his children.
4. Therefore, the children are entitled to the proceeds.

If Dana has any possible remedy at all, it would not be against the insurance proceeds themselves, and even any contract claim based on the oral promise would likely fail for the same Statute of Frauds reason. The proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

The key issues are:

1. whether Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to make Dana the beneficiary created an enforceable contractual right in Dana, and  
2. whether Lee’s post-marriage designation of Dana as beneficiary gave her a vested right that prevented a later change.

First, Lee’s oral promise is the kind of agreement that normally falls within the Statute of Frauds. A promise made “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary of my insurance” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage, not merely a mutual promise to marry. Agreements in consideration of marriage generally must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Here, no written antenuptial agreement was ever signed by Lee memorializing any promise to keep Dana as beneficiary. So Dana cannot enforce that promise as a contract.

Marriage itself does not usually take such an agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. If it did, the statutory requirement of a writing for marriage-based bargains would largely be defeated. So Dana’s performance—marrying Lee—does not by itself make the oral promise enforceable.

Second, Lee’s later act of naming Dana beneficiary after the wedding does not give her a vested right if the policy and by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries. In fraternal insurance associations, the member’s certificate is governed by the association’s charter and by-laws. Where those rules expressly reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries by a specified procedure, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property interest, until the member’s death. Dana’s rights therefore depended on Lee dying while she remained the designated beneficiary, unless she could point to a separate enforceable contract limiting Lee’s power to change the designation.

She cannot do that here, because the only alleged contract is the oral pre-marriage promise, and that is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. The standard beneficiary designation form Lee filed after marriage does not solve the problem. At most, it shows that Dana was then the current beneficiary. It does not appear to be a written agreement that Lee would keep her as beneficiary permanently or waive his by-law right to change the designation later. And because the by-laws were part of the arrangement, Dana took her designation subject to Lee’s reserved power to revoke it.

Lee later complied with the association’s prescribed procedure and validly changed the beneficiary designation to his children. Since Dana had no vested right and no enforceable contract preventing that change, the later designation controls.

Dana’s best argument would be for an equitable remedy—essentially, that Lee promised this in exchange for marriage and she relied on it. In some insurance cases, courts impose a constructive trust when an insured breaches an enforceable agreement to maintain someone as beneficiary. But that route depends on there being a valid underlying contract. Here there is not, because the alleged contract was oral and within the Statute of Frauds.

So the proceeds should go to the children, as the later validly designated beneficiaries.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage statement to Dana was an oral antenuptial promise: if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his insurance. A promise made in consideration of marriage is within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, no signed writing memorialized Lee’s promise. So Dana cannot enforce that promise as a contract.

Dana’s act of marrying Lee does not usually take the agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. If marriage itself were enough part performance, the statute’s marriage provision would be largely defeated. So her reliance by marrying him does not make the oral promise enforceable.

Nor does Lee’s later post-marriage designation of Dana as beneficiary give her a vested right to the proceeds. In a fraternal insurance certificate, where the association’s rules expressly permit the member to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a fixed property interest, until the insured dies. Lee later followed the association’s prescribed procedure and validly changed the beneficiary designation to his children. Unless Dana can point to an enforceable agreement that Lee would keep her as beneficiary, the later change controls.

The earlier designation of Dana does not cure the Statute of Frauds problem. It shows that Lee at one point carried out his oral promise, but because the certificate remained revocable under the by-laws, that designation was itself revocable. Without a valid written agreement restricting Lee’s right to change beneficiaries, Dana cannot convert that revocable designation into an irrevocable entitlement.

So Dana loses on both theories:
1. the premarital promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds; and
2. the post-marriage beneficiary designation gave her only a revocable expectancy, which Lee later displaced by a valid beneficiary change.

Accordingly, the children are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana has two possible theories: first, Lee’s oral promise before marriage; second, Lee’s post-marriage act of naming her as beneficiary. Neither gives her a superior right to the proceeds.

First, the pre-marriage promise is unenforceable. A promise made in consideration of marriage is within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the promisor. Lee’s statement—“if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary”—was exactly that kind of antenuptial promise. No signed writing memorialized it. Dana’s act of marrying Lee does not take the agreement out of the statute; marriage is the very consideration that triggers the writing requirement, and courts generally do not treat the marriage itself as part performance sufficient to enforce such an oral agreement.

Second, Dana did not acquire a vested right merely because Lee later filed the association’s form naming her beneficiary. In a fraternal insurance association, the certificate and the association’s by-laws govern. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. Where such a power exists, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a fixed property right, until the insured dies. So Dana’s status as beneficiary was revocable unless she could point to some separate enforceable contract making the designation irrevocable.

She cannot do that here. The only separate contract she relies on is the oral antenuptial promise, and that is barred by the Statute of Frauds. Also, if Lee’s promise is read literally as a promise merely to “make” her the beneficiary, he actually performed that promise when he named her after the marriage. Nothing in the facts shows a valid written agreement that he would keep her as beneficiary permanently.

Because Dana had only a revocable expectancy under the certificate, and because Lee validly exercised his contractual right under the by-laws to substitute his children, the later designation controls.

So the insurance proceeds belong to the children.

The children have the better claim.

The case turns on two rules:

1. An agreement made in consideration of marriage must generally be in writing to be enforceable.
2. In a fraternal benefit association, where the by-laws permit the member to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right, until the member dies.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise

Lee’s oral statement to Dana—“if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary”—was an antenuptial promise made upon consideration of marriage. Such promises fall within the Statute of Frauds and are unenforceable unless evidenced by a signed writing. Here, no signed writing memorialized that bargain.

Dana’s marriage does not cure that defect. In this setting, marriage is the consideration for the promise, not the kind of part performance that usually removes the agreement from the statute. So Dana cannot enforce the oral antenuptial promise as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Lee’s post-marriage designation of Dana

After the marriage, Lee did file the association’s form naming Dana as beneficiary. That gave her the status of beneficiary at that time. But under the association’s by-laws, Lee expressly retained the right to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. Therefore Dana did not obtain a vested contractual right to the proceeds merely from being named. She had only the rights of a beneficiary subject to Lee’s reserved power of substitution.

Could the designation form itself serve as the required writing? Probably not. It is a designation under the certificate, not a written contract with Dana that, in consideration of marriage, Lee would irrevocably keep her as beneficiary. Unless the form somehow contained language amounting to a contractual undertaking not to change beneficiaries—which the facts do not indicate—it does not satisfy the need for a signed antenuptial agreement.

Lee’s later change to the children

Several years later, Lee complied with the by-laws and validly changed the beneficiary designation to his two children. Because Dana had no vested right and the oral premarital promise is unenforceable, that later change controls. On Lee’s death, the proceeds belong to the beneficiaries then validly designated: the children.

Bottom line

Dana loses because her claim depends on an oral marriage-based promise barred by the Statute of Frauds, and her earlier beneficiary status was revocable under the association’s rules. The children, as the later properly substituted beneficiaries, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is the weak point in her case. A promise made “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, that kind of agreement must be in a signed writing, unless it is merely the mutual promise to marry. This was not just a promise to marry; it was an additional financial promise induced by marriage. Because no signed writing by Lee memorialized that bargain, Dana generally cannot enforce it as a contract.

Dana cannot avoid that result by arguing that she performed by marrying Lee. Marriage is the very consideration that brings the agreement within the Statute of Frauds, so the marriage itself ordinarily does not take the case out of the statute.

Her second argument—that Lee actually named her beneficiary after the wedding—also likely fails. That designation did make her the beneficiary at that time, but the certificate and by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing the required sworn request. Where the insured reserves that power, the named beneficiary has only an expectancy, not a vested right, until the insured dies. So Dana’s earlier designation did not freeze her rights or prevent Lee from later changing the beneficiary in compliance with the association’s rules.

The later change to the children appears valid because Lee followed the by-law procedure. Once he did that, the children became the beneficiaries entitled to the proceeds at Lee’s death.

Dana’s best possible reply would be that the post-marriage designation should count as a sufficient written memorandum of Lee’s premarital promise, or that equity should impose a constructive trust in her favor. But that argument is usually not enough here. The beneficiary form only shows that Lee designated Dana at one point; it does not memorialize an enforceable antenuptial contract that he would keep her as beneficiary irrevocably or for life. Given the express right to change beneficiaries, the form is more naturally read as a revocable designation, not as a binding contractual surrender of Lee’s power to change.

So, absent a signed antenuptial writing or some separate enforceable contract restricting Lee’s right to redesignate, Dana had only a revocable expectancy. The children, as the later validly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children likely have the better claim.

The key points are:

1. Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana was oral and was made in consideration of marriage.
2. Under the Statute of Frauds, an agreement made upon consideration of marriage generally must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged.
3. Dana’s later designation as beneficiary did not give her a vested right because the association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries.

Start with the pre-marriage promise. Lee orally told Dana that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. That is a promise made in consideration of marriage. Agreements of that kind ordinarily fall within the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds and are unenforceable unless reduced to a signed writing. Here, no written agreement was ever signed by Lee. So Dana cannot enforce the antenuptial promise itself as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Dana would argue that she performed by marrying Lee, so equity should enforce the promise. But courts generally do not treat the marriage itself as taking such an agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. Otherwise, the statute’s requirement for marriage-based agreements would largely be nullified. So her marriage does not cure the lack of a signed writing.

Next, consider the fact that after the marriage Lee did name Dana as beneficiary on the association’s standard form. That did give Dana the status of beneficiary at that time. But under the by-laws, Lee had the express right to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and he later complied with that procedure and named his two children. In fraternal benefit insurance, where the certificate and by-laws reserve the right to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property interest, until the insured dies. So Dana’s earlier designation did not freeze her rights or prevent Lee from making a valid later change.

Dana’s best theory would be an equitable one: that even though the association must pay according to the last valid designation, equity should impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in her favor because Lee had contracted to keep her as beneficiary. That theory can succeed in some cases where there is an enforceable agreement to maintain someone as beneficiary. But it fails here because the supposed underlying agreement is the oral pre-marriage promise, and that promise is barred by the Statute of Frauds. Without an enforceable contract, there is no equitable basis to override the later beneficiary change.

So the result is:

- As between the claimants, the children are entitled to the proceeds because they were the beneficiaries named in the last valid change made under the association’s rules.
- Dana cannot prevail based either on the oral antenuptial promise or on her earlier revocable designation.

Therefore, the children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to Dana is not enforceable as a contract to give her the insurance proceeds. A promise made “if you marry me, I will make you my beneficiary” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. That type of agreement falls within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, no signed writing memorialized Lee’s promise. Dana’s later performance by marrying Lee does not cure that problem, because marriage is the very consideration that brings the promise within the statute.

Dana therefore cannot enforce the oral antenuptial promise as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Nor does Lee’s post-marriage designation of Dana as beneficiary give her a superior right once he later changed the designation in accordance with the association’s rules. In a fraternal benefit or insurance certificate, the beneficiary’s rights are usually governed by the association’s charter, certificate, and by-laws. Where the by-laws expressly allow the member to change beneficiaries by following a stated procedure, the originally named beneficiary ordinarily has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested property right, until the insured dies without making a valid change.

That is what happened here. After the marriage, Lee named Dana beneficiary, but the association’s by-laws allowed later changes by sworn request. Lee later complied with that procedure and named his children. Because the right to change beneficiaries was reserved and validly exercised, Dana’s earlier designation was revoked.

Dana might argue that Lee’s act of naming her after the wedding partly performed or confirmed his promise. But at most that shows he initially carried out the promise in the form permitted by the certificate—namely, a revocable designation. It does not convert the oral premarital promise into an enforceable agreement that he would never change the beneficiary. Any such irrevocable obligation would still rest on the unenforceable oral marriage-based promise, and it would also conflict with the certificate structure under which beneficiary rights remained subject to change.

So Dana loses on both theories:
1. the oral premarital promise is barred by the Statute of Frauds; and
2. her later beneficiary status was defeasible and was effectively terminated when Lee validly changed the designation.

Accordingly, the children, as the later properly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is the weak point in Dana’s case. A promise made in consideration of marriage—other than a mutual promise to marry—falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s alleged promise was: “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary of my certificate.” That is an agreement upon consideration of marriage. Because no signed writing memorialized it, Dana generally cannot enforce it as a contract. The fact that she actually married Lee does not usually take such an agreement out of the Statute of Frauds.

Dana’s second argument, that Lee in fact did name her beneficiary after the marriage, also fails to give her a superior right to the proceeds. In a fraternal benefit or insurance association, the beneficiary’s rights are controlled by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. That means Dana’s designation was revocable and gave her only an expectancy, not a vested property right, during Lee’s lifetime. Since Lee later complied with the association’s prescribed method and validly named his two children as the new beneficiaries, the children became entitled to the proceeds when Lee died.

So Dana loses on both theories:

1. The antenuptial oral promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.
2. Her earlier beneficiary designation did not vest irrevocable rights because the by-laws allowed Lee to change beneficiaries, and he did so properly.

Accordingly, the association should pay the proceeds to the children.

At most, if there had been a valid written antenuptial agreement, Dana might have argued for an equitable claim against the proceeds or against Lee’s estate for breach of contract. But on these facts, without a signed writing and with a valid later change of beneficiary, the children prevail.

The children have the better claim.

Dana has two possible theories: (1) Lee’s oral premarital promise that if she married him he would make her beneficiary, and (2) Lee’s actual post-marriage designation of her on the association’s form. Neither is enough to defeat the children’s later designation.

First, the premarital promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. An agreement made upon consideration of marriage—other than the mutual promise to marry itself—must generally be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s alleged promise was exactly that: “marry me and I will make you beneficiary.” No signed writing memorialized that bargain. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually take such an agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So she cannot enforce the oral antenuptial promise as a contract.

Second, Lee’s later act of naming Dana beneficiary after the marriage did not give her a vested right to the proceeds. In a fraternal benefit or similar life-insurance arrangement, where the certificate and by-laws reserve to the member the power to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy until the insured dies. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later did exactly that, naming his children. Because Dana’s interest was revocable, Lee’s earlier designation of her did not lock in her rights.

Dana might argue that the post-marriage designation is evidence of the premarital agreement, or that equity should impose a constructive trust because she married in reliance on Lee’s promise. But that argument still runs into the same problem: the alleged underlying agreement was one in consideration of marriage and was not supported by a sufficient signed writing showing a contractual commitment to keep Dana as beneficiary. The beneficiary designation form merely named her at that time; it did not show an enforceable promise not to change her later. And absent an enforceable contract restricting Lee’s right to change beneficiaries, the association’s rules control.

So, because Lee validly exercised his reserved right to substitute beneficiaries, and because Dana’s premarital contract claim is barred by the Statute of Frauds, the proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

An oral promise made “if you marry me, I will make you my insurance beneficiary” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Such agreements fall within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, no written antenuptial agreement was ever signed by Lee. So Dana ordinarily cannot enforce that promise as a contract.

Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that problem. Marriage is the very consideration that brings the promise within the Statute of Frauds; it is not the kind of part performance that removes the agreement from the statute.

Nor does Lee’s post-marriage beneficiary designation solve it. That writing shows only that, at that time, he named Dana as beneficiary. It does not memorialize a binding contract that he would keep her as beneficiary and never change the designation. To satisfy the Statute of Frauds for the premarital bargain, the writing would need to evidence the contractual obligation; a routine beneficiary form usually does not do that.

That leaves Dana with only the rights she had as a named beneficiary under the certificate itself. But under the association’s by-laws, Lee expressly retained the power to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. He later exercised that power in the required manner and named his children. In fraternal benefit insurance, where the member reserves the right to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary generally has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right, until the member dies. After the wedding, Lee filed the association’s form naming Dana as beneficiary. That gave Dana whatever rights a named beneficiary normally has under the certificate and the association’s by-laws. But the only such contract she has is the oral premarital promise, and that is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

Accordingly, the children, as the later duly designated beneficiaries under the association’s rules, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s argument has two possible bases: (1) Lee’s oral premarital promise to make her beneficiary if she married him, and (2) Lee’s later act of naming her beneficiary after the marriage. Neither is enough to defeat the children’s later designation.

First, the premarital promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. A promise made “in consideration of marriage” must generally be in a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s promise was exactly that: “marry me and I will make you beneficiary.” No signed writing memorialized that agreement. Dana did marry Lee, but marriage itself ordinarily is not the kind of part performance that takes an antenuptial promise out of the statute, because marriage is the very consideration contemplated by the statute. So Dana cannot enforce the oral promise as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Second, Lee’s later designation of Dana as beneficiary did not give her a vested right in the certificate proceeds. In a fraternal insurance association, the member’s rights and the beneficiary’s rights are governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. That means Dana’s status as beneficiary was revocable unless she had some separate enforceable contract or equitable assignment preventing change. She did not. Her only alleged separate contract is the premarital oral promise, and that fails under the Statute of Frauds.

So when Lee later complied with the association’s prescribed procedure and named his children as the new beneficiaries, that change was effective. Dana had only an expectancy under the earlier designation, not a vested property right.

Accordingly, the proceeds should go to the children, not Dana.

The children have the better claim.

The key points are:

1. Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana was oral and made in consideration of marriage.
A promise “if you marry me, I will make you beneficiary” is an antenuptial agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Such agreements fall within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be in writing, signed by the party to be charged. Here, no writing signed by Lee memorialized that promise. So Dana cannot enforce the premarital promise as a binding contract.

Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that problem. In this context, the marriage is the consideration for the promise; it is not the sort of part performance that takes the agreement out of the Statute of Frauds.

2. Dana’s designation as beneficiary after the marriage did not give her a vested right.
After the marriage, Lee did name Dana as beneficiary on the association’s form. But the association’s by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request. In a fraternal insurance association, the certificate and the by-laws govern the rights of beneficiaries. When the member reserves the right to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested property interest.

So Dana’s being named beneficiary initially did not make her right to the proceeds fixed or irrevocable.

3. Lee later complied with the by-laws and changed the beneficiary to his children.
Lee followed the association’s required procedure and designated his children as the new beneficiaries. Since Dana had only an expectancy, and since Lee validly exercised his reserved power to change beneficiaries, the children became entitled to the proceeds unless Dana can show some superior equitable right.

4. Dana has no superior equitable right because the only alleged contract supporting it is unenforceable.
Sometimes a beneficiary can prevail despite a later change if the insured had made an enforceable contract, for valuable consideration, not to change the beneficiary. But Dana’s only basis for claiming such a contractual right is Lee’s oral antenuptial promise. Because that promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, she cannot use it to defeat the later valid change of beneficiary.

So, legally, Dana cannot rely either on:
- the oral premarital promise, because it is unenforceable; or
- her earlier designation, because it was revocable under the by-laws.

The children, as the beneficiaries validly named under the association’s rules at Lee’s death, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim.

Dana has two possible theories, and both are weak.

First, Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise is not enforceable as a contract. A promise made in consideration of marriage—other than the mutual promise to marry itself—falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s alleged promise was: “if you marry me, I will make you beneficiary.” That is a promise supported by marriage as consideration, so it had to be memorialized in a signed writing. It was not. Dana’s later marriage to Lee does not cure that defect; marriage is generally not treated as part performance that takes such an agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. So Dana cannot compel payment on the theory of an enforceable antenuptial contract.

Second, Lee’s post-marriage act of naming Dana beneficiary did not give her an irrevocable right to the proceeds. In a fraternal insurance association, the certificate and the association’s by-laws govern. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Where the insured reserves that power, a named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, until the insured’s death. So Dana’s earlier designation was revocable.

Lee later exercised the change-of-beneficiary power in the exact manner the by-laws required and named his two children. Once he did that, the children became the proper beneficiaries under the certificate.

Dana could prevail only if she could show some separate enforceable agreement that Lee would keep her as beneficiary and would not later change the designation. But the only such agreement presented is the oral premarital promise, and that is barred by the Statute of Frauds.

So the proceeds should go to the children, not Dana.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise to Dana is the weakest part of her case. A promise made in consideration of marriage—such as “marry me and I will make you beneficiary of my insurance”—is ordinarily within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the promisor. Here, there was no signed writing memorializing Lee’s promise. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually take that kind of agreement out of the statute, because marriage is the very consideration the statute contemplates. So Dana cannot enforce the oral antenuptial promise as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Dana’s second point—that Lee did in fact name her beneficiary after the marriage—also does not give her a superior right. In a fraternal insurance association, the member’s rights are governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Where, as here, the by-laws expressly permit the member to change beneficiaries by following a stated procedure, the initially named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right. Lee later complied with the by-laws and filed the proper sworn request changing the beneficiary designation to his children. That valid later change superseded Dana’s earlier designation.

Dana would have a stronger argument if she could show an enforceable separate contract by which Lee bound himself not to change the beneficiary, and equity might in some cases impose a constructive trust despite a later change. But that theory fails here because the only alleged agreement was the oral premarital promise, and that agreement is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

So, as between Dana and the children, the children are entitled to the proceeds under the valid last beneficiary designation.

The children have the better claim.

Lee’s pre-marriage promise to Dana is likely unenforceable because it was an oral promise made in consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, a contract made upon consideration of marriage—other than the mutual promise to marry—must generally be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s alleged promise was: “if you marry me, I will make you beneficiary.” That is exactly the kind of marriage-consideration agreement the statute requires to be written. No signed writing exists, so Dana cannot enforce that promise as a contract.

Dana’s actual marriage to Lee does not usually save the promise. Marriage is the very consideration that brings the agreement within the statute; it is not the sort of “part performance” that takes the contract out of it.

Dana also cannot prevail merely because Lee did, after the marriage, designate her as beneficiary. In a fraternal insurance association, where the certificate and by-laws reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries, the named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested right, until the member dies. Here the by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee later complied with that procedure and named his children. Once he validly made that change, Dana’s prior designation was revoked.

So Dana has no enforceable contract right because the premarital promise was oral and within the Statute of Frauds, and she has no vested beneficiary right because Lee validly exercised his reserved power to change beneficiaries.

Accordingly, the proceeds should go to the children.

The children have the better claim.

The key issue is whether Lee’s pre-marriage oral promise gave Dana an enforceable right to remain beneficiary despite Lee’s later change of beneficiary under the association’s by-laws.

First, Dana’s strongest argument is contractual: Lee told her before the marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary. She did marry him, and after the wedding he in fact named her. If that promise were enforceable, a court of equity might treat the later designation of the children as wrongful and impose a constructive trust on the proceeds for Dana.

But the main problem for Dana is that this was an antenuptial promise made in consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, an agreement made upon consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Here, no written agreement memorializing Lee’s promise was ever signed by him. So the promise that Dana would be made, and presumably kept, as beneficiary is unenforceable as a contract.

Dana cannot avoid that result by pointing out that she actually married Lee. Marriage is the very consideration that brings the agreement within the Statute of Frauds; it is not the kind of part performance that ordinarily takes such an agreement out of the statute. Nor does Lee’s later act of naming Dana as beneficiary satisfy the statute, because that designation was not itself a signed antenuptial contract obligating him to keep her as beneficiary. It merely showed that at that moment he chose her as beneficiary under the certificate.

Second, under the fraternal association’s by-laws, Lee retained the right to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and he later complied with that procedure. In such associations, where the member reserves the right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary generally has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, until the member dies. So Dana’s initial designation after marriage did not give her an irrevocable right to the proceeds.

Because Dana had only a revocable expectancy unless supported by an enforceable contract, and because the only alleged contract was an oral promise unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, Lee was free to change the beneficiary. He did so in the manner required by the by-laws, and the children were the beneficiaries at his death.

So, absent some separate valid written agreement not described here, the proceeds belong to the children.

The children have the better claim.

Dana’s strongest argument is not that she remained the named beneficiary at Lee’s death—she did not—but that Lee had made an enforceable contract before marriage to keep her as beneficiary. If that contract were enforceable, a court of equity could in some situations impose a constructive trust on the proceeds even though Lee later changed the beneficiary in accordance with the association’s rules.

But the problem for Dana is that Lee’s promise was oral and was made in consideration of marriage. An agreement made upon consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and generally must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, no written antenuptial agreement or other signed memorandum set out Lee’s promise that, in return for Dana’s marriage, he would make and keep her beneficiary. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not usually take such an oral marriage-based agreement out of the Statute of Frauds; otherwise the statute would be defeated in nearly every case of this kind.

Lee’s later filing of the association’s beneficiary form naming Dana does not materially improve her position. That form showed only that Dana was the beneficiary at that time. It did not memorialize a binding contract, made in consideration of marriage, that Lee would not later change the beneficiary. So it is not enough to satisfy the Statute of Frauds as to the alleged premarital bargain.

Absent an enforceable contract, Dana’s rights as beneficiary were only those given by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Those by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee later complied with that procedure and designated his children. Where the insured reserves the right to change the beneficiary, the earlier beneficiary has only a revocable expectancy, not a vested right in the proceeds. Thus Dana’s earlier designation gave her no fixed entitlement that survived Lee’s valid later change.

So, on these facts, the proceeds belong to the children as the beneficiaries validly designated at Lee’s death. Dana may feel morally wronged, but she has no superior legal claim to the insurance proceeds.

# Analysis of Competing Claims to Insurance Proceeds

This question presents a conflict between Dana's claim based on (1) an oral pre-marriage promise supported by the consideration of marriage and (2) Lee's initial post-marriage beneficiary designation, on the one hand, and the children's claim based on Lee's later valid beneficiary change, on the other. The resolution turns on several intersecting doctrines: the Statute of Frauds, the enforceability of agreements to fix a beneficiary irrevocably, the rights of a member under a fraternal benefit association's by-laws, and the effect of a proper beneficiary change.

## The Children Have the Better Claim

**1. Lee's Right to Change Beneficiaries Under the Association's By-Laws**

Fraternal benefit associations operate under their own constitutions and by-laws, which form part of the contract between the member and the association. Here, the by-laws expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed that procedure and named his two children. On its face, this change was procedurally valid. Under the general rule governing fraternal insurance, a member retains the power to change beneficiaries unless that power has been effectively and irrevocably relinquished. Because the by-laws authorized the change and Lee complied with the prescribed procedure, the children's designation is presumptively valid.

**2. Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise**

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—was supported by valid consideration (the marriage itself) and therefore created a binding contract that stripped Lee of the power to later change the beneficiary designation. If enforceable, this agreement would make Dana an irrevocable beneficiary, and the subsequent change to the children would be ineffective as against her equitable rights.

However, this argument encounters several serious obstacles.

**a. The Statute of Frauds**

Under the original Statute of Frauds and its modern codifications, a promise made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was entirely oral; no written agreement memorializing it was ever signed. Dana's claim therefore falls squarely within the marriage-consideration provision of the Statute of Frauds. The promise is unenforceable unless an exception applies.

Dana might argue partial performance or estoppel. She did, after all, marry Lee in reliance on the promise, and Lee did initially designate her as beneficiary. However, the marriage itself is generally considered insufficient part performance to take a promise out of the Statute of Frauds under the marriage-consideration provision, because the marriage is the very consideration contemplated—it does not constitute the kind of additional, unequivocal act that the part-performance doctrine typically requires (such as possession of land or making of improvements in real-property cases). Courts have historically been reluctant to apply part performance to the marriage provision with the same liberality as to the real-property provision. Moreover, Lee's act of initially naming Dana as beneficiary was not irrevocable under the by-laws and thus does not conclusively establish an intent to be permanently bound.

**b. No Enforceable Agreement to Make Dana an Irrevocable Beneficiary**

Even if the Statute of Frauds could somehow be overcome, Dana faces the additional problem that an agreement to designate someone as an irrevocable beneficiary of a life-insurance or fraternal-benefit certificate must be clearly established. Courts distinguish between (i) a mere designation of a beneficiary, which remains revocable under the policy or certificate terms, and (ii) a separate, enforceable contract supported by independent consideration in which the insured agrees never to change the beneficiary.

Here, the oral promise was general: Lee said he would "make her the beneficiary." He did not explicitly promise never to change the designation. Even construing the promise generously, the by-laws reserved to the member the right to change beneficiaries. A fraternal association's by-laws are incorporated into the certificate, and the member's rights under those by-laws generally cannot be contracted away by an oral side agreement—especially one that the association itself was not a party to and had no notice of. The association is entitled to pay proceeds to the last properly designated beneficiary, and it cannot be bound by a private oral agreement between the member and a claimant.

Dana could seek to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in the children's hands, arguing that Lee's change of beneficiary was a breach of his promise and that the children, as volunteers (they gave no consideration for the designation), should not be unjustly enriched. This is the equitable remedy courts sometimes apply when an insured breaches a valid contract to maintain a beneficiary designation. But this remedy presupposes the existence of an enforceable underlying contract—and here, the Statute of Frauds renders the oral promise unenforceable.

**c. Lee's Post-Marriage Designation of Dana Does Not Create Irrevocability**

The fact that Lee initially filed the form naming Dana as beneficiary does not independently help Dana. Under the association's by-laws, a beneficiary designation is inherently revocable. Lee's act of naming Dana was a unilateral exercise of his membership rights, not a contractual commitment to maintain her as beneficiary in perpetuity. A revocable beneficiary has no vested interest in the proceeds during the insured's lifetime; the beneficiary's interest is a mere expectancy that can be defeated at any time by a proper change.

**3. Policy Considerations and the Weight of Authority**

Courts have consistently held that where a fraternal association's by-laws permit beneficiary changes, a properly executed change controls unless the claimant can demonstrate an enforceable, typically written, agreement supported by consideration that the designation would be irrevocable. The law favors the insured's freedom to change beneficiaries and protects the association's ability to pay the last-named beneficiary without fear of competing claims based on undocumented oral promises.

Additionally, the Statute of Frauds' marriage-consideration provision exists precisely to prevent the kind of claim Dana is making—an oral promise allegedly made as an inducement to marriage, asserted after the promisor's death when the promisor cannot testify as to the terms or existence of the promise. The evidentiary and cautionary functions of the Statute are at their strongest in this context.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was a promise made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to a signed writing. Even apart from the Statute of Frauds, Dana cannot establish an enforceable agreement to make her an irrevocable beneficiary; the by-laws reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries, and Lee's initial designation of Dana created only a revocable expectancy. Lee exercised his right to change beneficiaries in compliance with the association's prescribed procedure, and the children, as the last properly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the proceeds.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem involves a conflict between Dana's claim based on (1) an oral pre-marriage promise supported by the consideration of marriage, and (2) Lee's initial designation of her as beneficiary, versus the children's claim based on Lee's subsequent valid change of beneficiary executed in compliance with the association's by-laws. The resolution requires analyzing contract law, the Statute of Frauds, the nature of beneficiary designations in fraternal insurance, and the doctrine of equitable estoppel.

## The Children's Claim Under the Later Beneficiary Change

The children's claim rests on straightforward grounds. Lee's fraternal insurance certificate was governed by the association's by-laws, which expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed that procedure. Under the general rule governing fraternal benefit societies and life insurance, a member retains the right to change beneficiaries at any time in accordance with the organization's rules, unless that right has been effectively and irrevocably surrendered. When a member complies with the prescribed procedure, the new designation supersedes the old one, and the most recent valid designation controls the distribution of proceeds at death. On these facts, Lee's designation of his two children as beneficiaries was the last valid designation on file, and it was executed in compliance with the by-laws.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's primary argument is that Lee's oral promise—made before the marriage—to name her as beneficiary constituted a binding contract supported by the consideration of her agreement to marry him. Marriage is recognized as valid consideration for a promise. Dana would argue that this contract, once formed, effectively divested Lee of the power to change the beneficiary designation, making her an irrevocable beneficiary whose rights could not be defeated by Lee's unilateral act.

### The Statute of Frauds Problem

Dana's claim faces a significant threshold obstacle. Under the Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. This requirement appears in the original English Statute of Frauds and is preserved in most American jurisdictions. Lee's promise was entirely oral; no written agreement memorializing it was ever signed by Lee. The facts emphasize this point. Accordingly, Lee's oral promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds unless an exception applies.

### Possible Exceptions to the Statute of Frauds

**Part Performance / Estoppel:** Dana could argue that her performance—actually marrying Lee—takes the oral agreement outside the Statute of Frauds. Under the part-performance doctrine, when a party has fully performed her side of the bargain in reliance on the oral promise, some courts will enforce the promise to prevent injustice. Dana did marry Lee, which constitutes full performance of her obligation under the alleged agreement. However, courts have historically been cautious about applying part performance to promises made in consideration of marriage, in part because the Statute of Frauds provision covering such promises was specifically designed to prevent fraudulent claims of this nature. Many jurisdictions hold that marriage alone, without additional acts of reliance beyond the marriage itself, is insufficient to invoke part performance as an exception. The marriage is the very consideration that triggers the Statute, and allowing it simultaneously to serve as the basis for circumventing the Statute would effectively nullify the provision.

**Equitable Estoppel:** Dana might argue that Lee should be estopped from denying the promise because she changed her position (by marrying him) in reliance on it. However, equitable estoppel arguments in this context face the same circularity problem: the reliance is the marriage itself, which is the consideration that brings the Statute of Frauds into play.

### Even If the Oral Promise Were Enforceable

Even assuming Dana could overcome the Statute of Frauds, she faces additional difficulties.

**The Nature of the Beneficiary's Interest in Fraternal Insurance:** In fraternal benefit societies, the prevailing rule is that a named beneficiary has no vested right in the certificate during the member's lifetime. The member's right to change the beneficiary is considered a personal privilege incident to membership. For a beneficiary designation to become irrevocable, there must typically be a clear, enforceable agreement—supported by consideration—in which the member surrenders the right to change the designation. Courts require strong evidence of such an agreement before they will override a later beneficiary change that was procedurally valid.

**Did Lee's Promise Create an Irrevocable Designation?** Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, constituted an agreement to irrevocably designate her as beneficiary, and that his subsequent act of naming her on the form was the fulfillment of that contractual obligation. If this contract were enforceable, the designation would arguably be coupled with a contractual obligation, transforming what would otherwise be a revocable designation into an irrevocable one.

However, the problem is that the underlying contract (the oral promise) is unenforceable due to the Statute of Frauds. Without an enforceable contract restricting Lee's right to change beneficiaries, Dana's designation was merely a revocable one—subject to change under the association's by-laws. Lee exercised that right. The subsequent act of naming Dana on the form, standing alone, was simply a revocable beneficiary designation, not an independent contract.

**Dana's Argument Based on the Designation Itself:** Dana might argue that even apart from the oral promise, Lee's act of naming her as beneficiary after the marriage created some enforceable right. But a standard beneficiary designation in a fraternal insurance association is revocable by its nature unless the certificate or by-laws provide otherwise or unless there is a separate enforceable agreement making it irrevocable. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed changes, and there was no enforceable written agreement restricting that power.

## The Equitable Argument

Dana could make a broader equitable argument: she married Lee in reliance on his promise, he initially honored that promise, and it would be unjust to allow his later unilateral change to defeat her expectation. Courts are sometimes sympathetic to such arguments, particularly where a spouse has been induced to marry based on financial promises. However, equity follows the law, and where the Statute of Frauds bars enforcement of the underlying promise, courts are generally reluctant to use equity to achieve indirectly what cannot be achieved directly—especially when the legislature has specifically required a writing for promises made in consideration of marriage.

Some courts have recognized a constructive trust theory in extreme cases of fraud or unjust enrichment. Dana could argue that the children would be unjustly enriched if they received proceeds that should have gone to her. But the children are not wrongdoers; they were simply named as beneficiaries by their father exercising a right the association's rules gave him. The unjust enrichment argument is weak on these facts.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was a promise made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to writing. Without an enforceable underlying contract, Dana's beneficiary designation was merely revocable, and Lee validly exercised his right under the association's by-laws to change the beneficiary to his children. The last valid beneficiary designation controls, and that designation names the children. While Dana's situation may evoke sympathy, the law requires that promises in consideration of marriage be memorialized in writing, and the absence of such a writing is fatal to her claim. The children, as the last-named beneficiaries under a procedurally proper change, are entitled to the proceeds.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to the Insurance Proceeds

This dispute requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the effect of the beneficiary designation and subsequent change under the fraternal association's by-laws, and whether Dana has any equitable basis to override the later designation.

## The Children's Claim Under the Later Beneficiary Designation

The children's claim rests on straightforward contractual grounds. A life-insurance certificate issued by a fraternal benefit association is a contract between the member and the association, governed by the association's charter and by-laws. The by-laws here expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee followed that procedure. Under well-established insurance law, where a member retains the right to change beneficiaries and exercises that right in compliance with the prescribed method, the last properly designated beneficiary is entitled to the proceeds. Lee's designation of his two children as beneficiaries, executed in accordance with the association's procedures, is presumptively valid and controlling.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's argument necessarily rests on the theory that Lee's oral promise—made before marriage, with marriage as the consideration—created an enforceable obligation that either (a) vested in her an irrevocable right to the proceeds, or (b) gives rise to an equitable claim (such as constructive trust) that should override the later designation.

### The Promise as an Antenuptial Agreement

Lee's oral statement that he would make Dana the beneficiary of his insurance certificate if she married him has the character of an antenuptial (prenuptial) agreement. Marriage is universally recognized as valid consideration, so the promise is not lacking in consideration. The critical question is whether it is enforceable despite never having been reduced to writing.

Most jurisdictions require antenuptial agreements to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds. Many states have specific statutes requiring prenuptial agreements to be written and signed (the Uniform Premarital Agreement Act, adopted in a majority of jurisdictions, explicitly requires a writing). Even under the traditional Statute of Frauds, an agreement made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing. Lee's oral promise falls squarely within this provision. Because no written agreement memorializing the promise was ever signed by Lee, the Statute of Frauds renders the oral antenuptial promise unenforceable at law.

Dana might argue part performance or estoppel to overcome the Statute of Frauds. She did marry Lee, and Lee did initially designate her as beneficiary—arguably performing the promise. However, the doctrine of part performance is traditionally applied to land contracts, and courts have been reluctant to extend it broadly to agreements in consideration of marriage. Moreover, even if one treats Lee's initial designation as performance, the question is whether that performance created a vested, irrevocable right—which leads to the next issue.

### Whether Lee's Initial Designation of Dana Created an Irrevocable Right

Even assuming the oral promise were somehow enforceable, Dana must show that it stripped Lee of the power to change beneficiaries. Under the law of fraternal benefit associations, the member's right to change beneficiaries is a contractual right conferred by the association's by-laws. A beneficiary under such a certificate generally holds a mere expectancy, not a vested right, so long as the by-laws reserve to the member the power of revocation.

For Dana to have acquired an irrevocable interest, there would need to be a valid, enforceable contract supported by consideration in which Lee agreed to designate her irrevocably—essentially surrendering his right to change beneficiaries. Courts have recognized that a member can contractually bind himself not to change a beneficiary designation, but the requirements are strict: the agreement must be enforceable (which typically means it must satisfy the Statute of Frauds and other formalities), and the consideration must be adequate. Here, the oral promise fails the writing requirement, and thus cannot serve as the basis for finding that Lee contractually divested himself of the right to change beneficiaries.

Furthermore, many fraternal association by-laws and governing statutes provide that the member's right to change beneficiaries cannot be waived or limited by a private side agreement. If such a provision applies here, Dana's claim would fail on that independent ground as well.

### Equitable Arguments: Constructive Trust and Estoppel

Dana might seek imposition of a constructive trust on the proceeds, arguing that Lee's change of beneficiary after inducing her to marry him on the strength of his promise constitutes fraud or unjust enrichment. Courts have occasionally imposed constructive trusts on insurance proceeds where a beneficiary change was procured by fraud, undue influence, or in violation of an enforceable agreement. However, the threshold requirement is that the underlying obligation be enforceable. An oral promise that violates the Statute of Frauds generally cannot serve as the foundation for a constructive trust, because to allow it would effectively nullify the statute. Courts have consistently held that the Statute of Frauds cannot be circumvented simply by recharacterizing the claim as equitable.

Dana might also invoke promissory estoppel, arguing she relied to her detriment on Lee's promise by entering into the marriage. While promissory estoppel can sometimes override the Statute of Frauds where injustice would otherwise result, courts have been cautious about applying it in the marriage-consideration context. The marriage itself conferred benefits on Dana (marital rights, potential support, property rights, etc.), and courts are generally reluctant to find that marriage alone constitutes the kind of unconscionable injury that justifies estoppel. Moreover, Lee did designate her initially, and Dana enjoyed the status of beneficiary for several years; the fact that the designation was later changed does not necessarily establish the kind of unjust reliance injury that estoppel requires.

### The Significance of Lee's Compliance with Association Procedures

A critical practical point is that the fraternal association is obligated to pay the proceeds to the beneficiary designated in accordance with its by-laws. The association's contract is with Lee, and the by-laws constitute the governing terms. Lee complied with the by-laws when he changed the beneficiary to his children. The association would face liability if it paid Dana in contravention of the last valid designation. Even if Dana had some equitable claim against Lee's estate, that claim would not bind the association or redirect the proceeds absent a court order.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as an agreement made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to writing. Without an enforceable agreement, Dana cannot establish that Lee's initial designation of her as beneficiary was irrevocable. Lee retained the contractual right under the association's by-laws to change his beneficiary, and he exercised that right in full compliance with the prescribed procedures. Dana's equitable arguments—constructive trust and promissory estoppel—are unlikely to succeed because they rest on an oral promise barred by the Statute of Frauds, and because courts are reluctant to use equity to circumvent that statutory requirement. The last valid beneficiary designation controls, and the children, as the properly designated beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death, are entitled to the proceeds.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem presents a conflict between Dana, who claims the proceeds based on (1) Lee's oral pre-marriage promise and (2) his initial post-marriage beneficiary designation, and Lee's two children, who claim under the later beneficiary change executed in compliance with the association's by-laws. The children have the better claim, though the analysis requires careful consideration of several doctrines.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's strongest theoretical argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—supported by the consideration of her agreement to marry him, created a binding contract that should be specifically enforced or that should estop Lee (or his children as successors) from changing the beneficiary designation.

**Consideration.** Marriage is valid consideration for a promise. Dana's agreement to marry Lee in exchange for the beneficiary designation would satisfy the consideration requirement of contract formation. Courts have long recognized that marriage constitutes sufficient consideration to support a contractual obligation, distinguishing such promises from gratuitous undertakings.

**Statute of Frauds.** However, Dana faces a formidable obstacle. Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was entirely oral, and no written agreement memorializing it was ever signed by Lee. The facts are explicit on this point. Dana would need to overcome the Statute of Frauds to enforce the oral promise.

Dana might argue part performance or estoppel to avoid the Statute of Frauds. She did, after all, marry Lee in reliance on the promise, and Lee did initially perform by naming her as beneficiary. Some jurisdictions recognize that where a party has fully performed under an oral agreement (here, Dana performed by marrying Lee), the Statute of Frauds should not bar enforcement. However, the weight of authority on promises in consideration of marriage tends to enforce the writing requirement strictly, particularly because the marriage itself is not unambiguously referable to the insurance promise—people marry for many reasons, and the act of marrying does not, standing alone, point unmistakably to the existence of an agreement regarding insurance proceeds. Courts applying the "unequivocal referability" test for part performance would likely find Dana's marriage insufficient to take the oral promise outside the statute.

**Even if enforceable, the promise may have been fulfilled.** Lee promised to "make her the beneficiary," and he did exactly that after the marriage by filing the standard form. One could argue the promise was fully performed at that point. The promise, by its terms, was to "make" Dana the beneficiary—not to keep her as beneficiary irrevocably and permanently. This is a plausible interpretation that would further undermine Dana's claim.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Initial Beneficiary Designation
