Dana might further argue that the oral agreement, once performed by the actual filing of the beneficiary designation, created in her a vested equitable interest in the proceeds. Courts have sometimes held that when a policyholder contractually agrees to name and maintain a particular beneficiary, an equitable lien or equitable assignment arises in favor of that person, enforceable against later-named beneficiaries who gave no consideration. Under this theory, even though the association's records show the children as the named beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death, Dana's equitable interest takes priority because the children are mere volunteers—they paid no consideration and occupy a position analogous to donee beneficiaries of the later change.

**3. Constructive Trust**

Relatedly, Dana could seek imposition of a constructive trust on the proceeds in the children's hands, arguing that it would be unjust enrichment for the children to receive proceeds that Lee had contractually promised to Dana.

## The Children's Arguments

**1. The Statute of Frauds**

The children's most formidable defense is the Statute of Frauds. Under the traditional common-law rule, codified in many jurisdictions, a contract made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise—"If you marry me, I will make you my beneficiary"—is precisely the type of agreement that falls within this provision. Lee never signed any written memorandum of this promise. Therefore, the children would argue, the oral promise is unenforceable.

Dana's response would be that the Statute of Frauds is satisfied (or rendered inapplicable) by full performance or part performance. Dana fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee, and Lee himself performed by actually filing the beneficiary designation. Many courts have held that full performance by one party takes an oral agreement out of the Statute of Frauds, or at least makes it enforceable in equity. The marriage itself constitutes a change of position so substantial and irrevocable that it would be a fraud to allow the promisor (or those claiming through him) to invoke the Statute of Frauds as a shield. Several jurisdictions recognize that where marriage has been performed in reliance on the promise, the marriage-provision of the Statute of Frauds does not bar enforcement. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 129 also supports enforcement where there has been reasonable reliance and performance that would make injustice unavoidable if the promise were not enforced.

**2. The Association's By-Laws and the Right to Change Beneficiaries**

The children will argue that the insurance certificate is a contract between Lee and the fraternal association, not between Lee and Dana. The association's by-laws expressly permitted Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee complied with every procedural requirement. As between the association and its members, the last properly filed beneficiary designation controls. The children, as the last-named beneficiaries, are entitled to the proceeds under the plain terms of the insurance contract.

This argument has real force in determining the association's obligation—the association would be protected in paying the children. However, the majority of courts distinguish between the association's contractual obligation and the equitable rights between competing claimants. The fact that the association's by-laws permit a change of beneficiary does not resolve the separate question of whether Lee was contractually bound to Dana not to exercise that power.

**3. Dana Was a Mere Donee Beneficiary Subject to Revocation**

The children might argue that Dana, as a named beneficiary, had only a revocable expectancy—not a vested right—and that Lee's later change simply extinguished that expectancy. However, this argument conflates two distinct sources of Dana's rights. If Dana were merely a gratuitously named beneficiary, her interest would indeed be revocable. But Dana claims not under the beneficiary designation alone; she claims under an independent contract supported by the consideration of marriage. It is the underlying contract, not the mere designation, that distinguishes her from an ordinary revocable beneficiary.

## The Prevailing View and Resolution

The weight of authority supports Dana's claim, subject to the Statute of Frauds analysis.

**The majority rule** in American jurisdictions is that where an insured enters into a valid contract, supported by consideration, to name and maintain a particular person as beneficiary, the insured may not thereafter defeat that obligation by changing the beneficiary designation. The later-named beneficiary takes nothing—or, more precisely, takes the proceeds subject to a constructive trust or equitable lien in favor of the person with the contractual right. The rationale is straightforward: the insured cannot do indirectly (breach a contract by changing the beneficiary) what he could not do directly (refuse to perform the contract). The later-named beneficiaries, being mere volunteers who provided no consideration for the change, have no equitable standing to defeat the prior contractual claimant.

Courts applying this rule have specifically held that the power to change beneficiaries reserved in the policy or certificate is a power as between the insured and the insurer, but that power can be circumscribed by the insured's separate contractual obligations to third parties. As between competing claimants, equity will enforce the prior contract.

**On the Statute of Frauds**, the better-reasoned view—and the one adopted by most courts confronting this specific pattern—is that the marriage-consideration provision of the Statute of Frauds does not bar enforcement where the marriage has been performed. Full performance of the marriage removes the principal danger the Statute was designed to prevent (fabricated claims of marital promises), and the irreversibility of the marriage makes it unconscionable to permit the defense. Furthermore, Lee's own act of filing the beneficiary designation corroborates the existence of the oral promise and constitutes an admission by conduct. Some courts have also applied promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139) to enforce such promises where injustice would otherwise result, even without full satisfaction of the Statute.

**The children's position** is not without sympathy—Lee may have had legitimate reasons for wanting to provide for his children from the earlier marriage. But the legal question is not what Lee wanted at the time of the change; it is whether he had the legal power to make that change given his prior contractual commitment. Having induced Dana to marry him with a promise of the insurance proceeds, and having performed that promise, Lee could not unilaterally undo the bargain. The children, as volunteers, stand in no better position than Lee himself would have stood had he tried to recover the proceeds during his lifetime.

## Conclusion

Dana has the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral promise, supported by the valid consideration of marriage and corroborated by his subsequent performance in actually naming Dana as beneficiary, created a binding contractual obligation that Lee could not unilaterally abrogate by later changing the beneficiary designation. Although the fraternal association would be justified in paying the named beneficiaries of record (the children), equity will impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in the children's hands for Dana's benefit. The Statute of Frauds does not bar Dana's claim because the marriage has been fully performed, making it inequitable to invoke the statute, and Lee's own act of filing the designation serves as corroborating conduct. The children, having furnished no consideration for the beneficiary change, are volunteers who take subject to Dana's prior equitable and contractual right.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem presents a conflict between Dana, who claims the proceeds based on (1) Lee's oral pre-marriage promise and (2) his initial post-marriage beneficiary designation, and Lee's two children from a prior marriage, who claim under the later beneficiary change executed in accordance with the association's by-laws. The children have the better claim, though the analysis requires working through several doctrines.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's strongest theoretical argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary of my life-insurance certificate"—supported by the consideration of her marriage, created a binding contract that gave her a vested equitable right in the policy proceeds that Lee could not later revoke by changing the beneficiary designation.

**Consideration.** Marriage is valid consideration for a promise. Dana's agreement to marry Lee in exchange for being named beneficiary satisfies the bargain requirement. Courts have long recognized that marriage constitutes sufficient consideration to support a contract, and a promise made in contemplation of marriage, where the marriage is induced by the promise, is enforceable on consideration grounds—at least in principle.

**The Statute of Frauds, however, presents a formidable obstacle.** Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was oral, and no written agreement memorializing it was ever signed by Lee. The facts are explicit on this point. Dana's promise falls squarely within the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds. Because the promise was oral and Lee never signed a memorandum of it, the Statute of Frauds renders the promise unenforceable unless an exception applies.

**Part performance and estoppel arguments.** Dana might argue that her performance—actually marrying Lee—takes the oral agreement outside the Statute of Frauds under the doctrine of part performance or promissory estoppel. Courts are divided on whether marriage alone constitutes sufficient part performance to overcome the Statute of Frauds in this context. Some jurisdictions hold that merely going through with the marriage is not the kind of part performance (typically associated with land contracts involving possession, improvements, and payment) that equity will recognize. Other courts, particularly older ones, have been more sympathetic where the promisee has changed her position irrevocably by marrying. Even in jurisdictions that might recognize part performance, the doctrine is applied cautiously, and the mere fact of marriage—which Dana presumably desired for reasons beyond the insurance proceeds—may not be deemed sufficient to invoke equitable relief. The promissory estoppel argument faces similar difficulties: Dana must show detrimental reliance that is unconscionable to leave unredressed, and courts are reluctant to find that the marriage itself constitutes the kind of reliance injury that demands enforcement of the oral promise.

On balance, the Statute of Frauds likely bars enforcement of the oral pre-marriage promise in most jurisdictions.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Post-Marriage Beneficiary Designation

After the marriage, Lee did in fact file the association's standard form naming Dana as beneficiary. Dana might argue that this designation, combined with the underlying oral promise, created an irrevocable beneficiary designation—essentially that Lee, having performed his promise, could not later undo it.

**The nature of the beneficiary designation under the association's by-laws.** The critical fact is that the association's by-laws expressly allowed a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. This means that, under the terms of the insurance certificate (which is a contract between Lee and the association), Dana was a *revocable* beneficiary. A revocable beneficiary has no vested right in the policy proceeds; the beneficiary's interest is a mere expectancy that can be defeated at any time by the insured's exercise of the reserved power to change beneficiaries.

**Did the oral promise convert Dana from a revocable to an irrevocable beneficiary?** This is the heart of Dana's argument: even though the policy terms made her designation revocable, the separate oral contract (supported by the consideration of marriage) stripped Lee of the power to change the designation. In effect, Dana argues that Lee held the power to change beneficiaries in trust for her, or that equity should treat her designation as irrevocable.

Some courts have recognized this theory. Where a policyholder makes a *valid, enforceable* contract with a third party to name and maintain that person as beneficiary, and the contract is supported by consideration, equity may impose a constructive trust on the proceeds if the policyholder breaches by changing the beneficiary. The key requirement, however, is that the underlying promise must be **enforceable**. This brings us back to the Statute of Frauds problem. If the oral promise is unenforceable because it falls within the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds and no exception applies, then there is no enforceable contract to serve as the basis for a constructive trust or equitable lien on the proceeds.

**Could the act of filing the beneficiary form itself constitute a writing satisfying the Statute of Frauds?** This is a creative argument, but it likely fails. The beneficiary designation form is a document between Lee and the association; it does not memorialize the terms of the alleged agreement between Lee and Dana (i.e., that in exchange for marriage, Dana would be named and *maintained* as beneficiary). The form simply names a beneficiary pursuant to the policy's standard procedures. It does not evidence a contractual obligation to keep Dana as beneficiary, which is the essential term Dana needs to enforce.

## The Children's Claim Under the Later Beneficiary Change

Lee followed the association's prescribed procedure for changing beneficiaries: he filed a sworn request naming his two children as the new beneficiaries. Under well-established insurance law, when the insured has a reserved right to change the beneficiary and complies substantially with the procedure specified in the policy or by-laws, the change is effective. The children's designation as beneficiaries was the last valid designation in effect at Lee's death.

**The association's obligation.** Fraternal benefit associations are governed by their by-laws and certificates. The association's by-laws authorized the change, Lee complied with the procedure, and the association presumably accepted the change. The association is obligated to pay the proceeds to the designated beneficiaries as of the date of death—here, the children.

**No superior equitable claim by Dana.** For Dana to overcome the children's claim, she would need to establish an enforceable agreement that gave her an equitable right superior to the children's legal right as named beneficiaries. As analyzed above, the oral promise is likely barred by the Statute of Frauds, and without an enforceable underlying agreement, no constructive trust can be imposed on the proceeds.

## Additional Considerations

**Public policy regarding freedom to change beneficiaries.** Courts generally protect the insured's right to change beneficiaries, as this flexibility is a fundamental feature of life insurance. Restricting this right based on oral promises—particularly oral promises that fall within the Statute of Frauds—would undermine the certainty that insurance law demands and would invite fraudulent claims after the insured's death, when the insured can no longer testify about the alleged promise.

**The parol evidence concern.** Even setting aside the Statute of Frauds, courts are reluctant to allow oral testimony to override written instruments, particularly after the death of one of the parties. The beneficiary designation form and the later change form are written documents that speak clearly to the association's obligations. Allowing Dana to contradict these documents with testimony about an oral promise would raise serious concerns about reliability and fraud.

**Community property or marital property considerations.** In some jurisdictions, if the insurance premiums were paid with marital funds, Dana might have a claim to some portion of the proceeds as marital property. However, the problem does not raise this issue, and fraternal benefit certificates are often treated differently from commercial insurance policies in this regard. Moreover, any such claim would be based on marital property law, not on the oral promise, and would typically entitle Dana to only a portion of the proceeds, not the entirety.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was a promise made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to a writing signed by Lee. Without an enforceable underlying agreement, Dana cannot establish an equitable right (such as a constructive trust) that would override the children's status as the last-named beneficiaries under a valid beneficiary change executed in compliance with the association's by-laws. The association's by-laws expressly reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries, Lee exercised that right properly, and the children were the designated beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death. Dana's initial designation was revocable, and it was in fact revoked. The proceeds should be paid to the children.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem raises several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of antenuptial (premarital) oral promises, the nature of a beneficiary's interest in fraternal insurance, and the power of a member to change beneficiaries under an association's by-laws.

## Dana's Potential Theories

Dana can advance two arguments: (1) Lee's oral premarital promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, created an enforceable contract that vested in her an equitable right to the proceeds; and (2) Lee's subsequent designation of her as beneficiary, having been made and having remained in place for some time, gave her a vested interest that could not later be defeated.

**The Oral Premarital Promise.** Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's promise to name her as beneficiary, made in exchange for her agreement to marry him, constituted a contract supported by valid consideration. Marriage is universally recognized as sufficient consideration to support a promise. Under this theory, even if Lee later changed the beneficiary designation, Dana would hold an equitable interest in the proceeds that equity would enforce against the named beneficiaries (the children), who are essentially donee beneficiaries and thus volunteers with no superior equitable claim.

However, this theory faces a critical obstacle: the **Statute of Frauds**. Under the traditional English Statute of Frauds and its American counterparts, a promise made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was entirely oral, and no written memorandum was ever signed by him. The filing of the beneficiary-designation form after marriage, while a writing, is not typically treated as a memorandum of the premarital agreement because it does not recite the terms of the antenuptial bargain or acknowledge the obligation. It is merely the standard association form. Therefore, the Statute of Frauds would bar enforcement of the oral premarital promise as a contract.

Dana might argue **part performance** as a way around the Statute of Frauds—she performed her side by marrying Lee, and Lee partially performed by actually filing the designation. Some courts have recognized that where the marriage has been performed and the promisee has changed position irrevocably, the Statute of Frauds should not shield the promisor (or his estate/successors). However, the weight of authority holds that marriage alone, while it is performance of the promisee's obligation, does not constitute the kind of part performance (typically requiring acts like taking possession or making improvements, drawn from the real-property context) that takes an oral agreement outside the Statute. The part-performance doctrine is applied cautiously outside the land-contract setting, and many jurisdictions would not extend it here.

Dana might also invoke **equitable estoppel or fraud**, arguing that Lee induced her to marry him by a promise he never intended to keep, and that the Statute of Frauds should not be used as an instrument of fraud. This is a recognized exception, but Dana would need to show that Lee made the promise with the intent not to perform it—mere subsequent change of mind is generally insufficient. The facts suggest Lee did initially perform (he named her), and only later changed his mind. That undercuts the fraud theory.

**The Earlier Beneficiary Designation.** Dana's second argument is that once Lee designated her as beneficiary, she acquired a vested right that Lee could not unilaterally revoke. This argument fails under well-established insurance law. In fraternal benefit associations, the member's right to change beneficiaries is governed by the association's by-laws, which form part of the insurance contract. Here, the by-laws expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee followed that procedure. Where the certificate or by-laws reserve to the member the right to change beneficiaries, the designated beneficiary has a **mere expectancy**, not a vested right. The beneficiary's interest does not vest until the insured's death, and then it vests in whichever beneficiary is designated at that time. Since Lee properly exercised his contractual right to change beneficiaries, Dana's designation was effectively revoked.

The only way Dana could overcome this principle is if she could show that Lee's designation of her was made **for valuable consideration** and was therefore irrevocable. This brings the analysis back to the oral premarital promise. If the promise were enforceable, a court in equity could treat the designation as having been made pursuant to a binding obligation, rendering it irrevocable despite the by-laws' general permission to change. But as discussed above, the oral promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, so there is no underlying contractual obligation to anchor an irrevocability argument.

## The Children's Claim

The children are the last-designated beneficiaries. Lee followed the association's prescribed procedure—filing a sworn request—to name them. They hold the status of properly designated beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death. Under standard fraternal-insurance law, the proceeds are payable to the beneficiary of record at the member's death. The children, as the last properly designated beneficiaries, have a **legal right** to the proceeds.

The children are, admittedly, volunteer (donee) beneficiaries rather than beneficiaries for value. Ordinarily, a volunteer beneficiary's claim is subordinate to a prior equitable claimant who gave value. But that principle only helps Dana if she can establish a valid equitable claim—which, for the reasons above, she cannot.

## Conclusion

**The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds.** Lee's oral premarital promise to Dana, though supported by the consideration of marriage, is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was never reduced to a writing signed by Lee. Without an enforceable underlying contract, Dana's designation as beneficiary gave her only an expectancy, not a vested or irrevocable interest. Lee retained the power under the association's by-laws to change beneficiaries, and he properly exercised that power by naming his children through the prescribed procedure. At Lee's death, the children were the duly designated beneficiaries, and the association is obligated to pay the proceeds to them. Dana's equitable arguments—part performance, estoppel, and constructive trust—are unlikely to prevail given the clear applicability of the Statute of Frauds and the absence of evidence of fraud in the inducement. The children, as the last properly designated beneficiaries, are entitled to the proceeds.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem presents a conflict between Dana, who claims the proceeds based on (1) Lee's oral pre-marriage promise and (2) his initial post-marriage beneficiary designation, and Lee's two children from a prior marriage, who claim under the later beneficiary-change form. The analysis requires examining several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of the oral promise, the nature of a beneficiary's interest under a fraternal benefit certificate, and the member's power to change beneficiaries under the association's by-laws.

## The Children Have the Better Claim

**Lee's Power to Change Beneficiaries Under the Certificate and By-Laws**

A fraternal benefit association's insurance certificate is governed by the association's charter, constitution, and by-laws, which are incorporated into the certificate by operation of law. Here, the by-laws expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed that procedure exactly. Under well-established insurance law, where the certificate and by-laws reserve to the member the right to change the beneficiary, the named beneficiary holds only an expectancy—not a vested right—during the member's lifetime. The member may exercise the reserved power at any time, for any reason, and the last properly designated beneficiary prevails. Because Lee complied with the association's prescribed procedure for changing beneficiaries, the children, as the last-designated beneficiaries, hold the contractual right to the proceeds under the certificate itself.

**Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise**

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—supported by the consideration of marriage, created an enforceable contract that should be treated as an irrevocable designation, effectively estopping Lee (or his estate) from allowing the later change to stand.

There are several obstacles to this claim:

*Statute of Frauds.* In most jurisdictions, a promise made in consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee never signed any written agreement memorializing this promise. Dana would therefore need to overcome the Statute of Frauds, which is a significant hurdle. Some jurisdictions recognize part performance or estoppel as exceptions. Dana could argue that her marriage to Lee and Lee's subsequent designation of her as beneficiary constitute part performance sufficient to take the oral agreement outside the statute. Courts have sometimes accepted this reasoning, but the doctrine is applied cautiously, and the sufficiency of the part performance is debatable—particularly because the marriage itself is the very consideration for the promise, and allowing it alone to satisfy the part-performance exception would effectively swallow the rule for all marriage-consideration promises.

*Parol Evidence and the Nature of the Insurance Contract.* Even if the oral promise were enforceable between Lee and Dana, the insurance certificate is a contract between Lee and the fraternal association. The association is not a party to the oral agreement and is bound only by its own by-laws and the last properly filed beneficiary designation. Dana's claim, if cognizable at all, would sound as a claim against Lee's estate for breach of contract or in equity to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds—not as a direct claim against the association under the certificate.

*The Revocable-Beneficiary Doctrine.* The critical issue is whether an oral promise, even if otherwise enforceable, can override the member's reserved power to change beneficiaries. The majority rule is that a beneficiary designation under a life-insurance policy or fraternal certificate that is revocable by its terms remains revocable notwithstanding a separate oral or even written agreement between the insured and the beneficiary. The rationale is that the insurance contract is between the member and the insurer (or association), and the member's reserved right to change beneficiaries is a contractual power that cannot be extinguished by a side agreement to which the insurer is not a party—at least not so as to bind the insurer. The proper remedy, if any, lies in equity against the proceeds after they are paid.

**The Equitable Argument: Constructive Trust**

Dana's most viable theory would be to seek imposition of a constructive trust on the proceeds in the children's hands, arguing that Lee's change of beneficiary was a breach of his contractual obligation to her and that the children, as volunteer beneficiaries (they gave no consideration for their designation), should not be permitted to benefit from Lee's breach. Courts have occasionally imposed constructive trusts in such circumstances, but typically only where:

1. There is clear and convincing evidence of the agreement (here, the promise is oral and uncorroborated by any writing);
2. The agreement is enforceable (here, the Statute of Frauds problem persists);
3. The change of beneficiary was procured by fraud, undue influence, or was otherwise unconscionable (no such facts appear here); and
4. The claimant's equities clearly outweigh those of the new beneficiaries.

On these facts, Dana faces difficulty on nearly every element. The promise is oral and unwritten, implicating the Statute of Frauds. There is no evidence of fraud or undue influence in Lee's decision to change beneficiaries. The children, while donee beneficiaries, are Lee's own children from a prior marriage—they are natural objects of his bounty, not strangers. Courts are generally reluctant to impose constructive trusts based solely on oral promises to maintain a beneficiary designation, particularly where the insured had an unrestricted contractual right to make the change.

**Dana's Designation Was Itself Revocable**

It is also important to note that even Lee's act of naming Dana as beneficiary after the marriage does not help her claim under the certificate. That designation, like any beneficiary designation under a policy with a reserved right to change, was inherently revocable. It gave Dana no vested interest. The later change extinguished whatever expectancy she had under the certificate.

**Policy Considerations**

Allowing Dana to prevail on an oral promise would undermine the certainty that fraternal associations and insurers need in administering benefit payments. The by-laws establish a clear, formal mechanism for designating and changing beneficiaries precisely so that the association can pay proceeds to the correct party without being embroiled in disputes over alleged oral side agreements. Honoring the last properly filed designation serves the interests of administrative efficiency and predictability.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee exercised his reserved right to change beneficiaries in full compliance with the association's by-laws, and the children are the last properly designated beneficiaries. Dana's claim based on the oral pre-marriage promise is likely barred by the Statute of Frauds, and even if it were not, the majority rule holds that a revocable beneficiary designation may be changed notwithstanding a private agreement between the insured and a prior beneficiary. Dana's equitable claim for a constructive trust, while theoretically available, faces formidable obstacles given the absence of a writing, the absence of fraud or overreaching, and the children's status as natural objects of Lee's bounty. The association should pay—and a court should award—the proceeds to the children.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to Insurance Proceeds

This question requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the effect of the beneficiary designation and subsequent change under the fraternal association's by-laws, and the extent to which Dana can assert an equitable or contractual right that overrides the association's standard procedures.

## Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Lee orally promised Dana before their marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana's act of marrying Lee constitutes consideration for that promise—marriage is classic and well-recognized consideration for a contract. The question is whether this oral promise is enforceable despite the absence of a written agreement.

Under the Statute of Frauds, contracts made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) generally must be in writing to be enforceable. Lee's promise—"if you marry me, I will make you my beneficiary"—is precisely the type of agreement the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds was designed to cover. Because no written agreement memorializing this promise was ever signed by Lee, the promise is unenforceable at law under the Statute of Frauds.

However, Dana might argue that the Statute of Frauds is satisfied by part performance or should be overcome by equitable estoppel. Dana fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee, and Lee himself partially performed by actually filing the beneficiary designation naming Dana. Courts in some jurisdictions have held that where the promisee has fully performed and the promisor has partially performed, the Statute of Frauds does not bar enforcement. The doctrine of equitable estoppel or part performance could arguably remove the oral promise from the Statute's bar, particularly because Dana changed her position irrevocably by entering into the marriage.

## The Beneficiary Designation and the Power to Change

Even assuming Dana can overcome the Statute of Frauds problem, she faces a second significant obstacle: the fraternal association's by-laws expressly permitted Lee to change his beneficiary designation at any time by filing a sworn request. Lee followed this procedure and named his two children as the new beneficiaries. Under standard insurance law principles, a policyholder who retains the right to change beneficiaries may do so freely, and the named beneficiary at the time of death is entitled to the proceeds. A beneficiary designation is generally revocable unless the beneficiary has a **vested** right that cannot be unilaterally altered.

Under the majority rule, a named beneficiary in a life-insurance policy where the insured retains the right to change beneficiaries has a **mere expectancy**, not a vested right. The beneficiary's interest does not vest until the insured's death, and the insured may change the designation at will. Under this principle, Lee's later designation of his children would control, and Dana would have no claim based solely on the earlier designation.

## Dana's Theory: Irrevocable Beneficiary Designation Through Contract

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, created a **contract** that effectively made her designation **irrevocable**—that is, Lee's promise not only obligated him to name her as beneficiary but also implicitly obligated him not to change that designation. Under this theory, even though the association's by-laws permitted changes, Lee had contractually bound himself to Dana not to exercise that right. Dana would seek to impose a **constructive trust** on the proceeds in the hands of the children, arguing that Lee breached his contractual obligation and that the children, as volunteers (they gave no consideration for being named), should not be permitted to benefit from that breach.

Courts have recognized this theory in analogous cases. Where an insured makes an enforceable agreement—typically in a divorce settlement or separation agreement—to maintain a particular beneficiary designation, and then breaches that agreement by changing the beneficiary, courts have imposed constructive trusts on the proceeds in favor of the intended beneficiary. The key requirements are: (1) an enforceable agreement supported by consideration, (2) breach of that agreement by changing the beneficiary, and (3) the new beneficiaries are volunteers rather than bona fide purchasers for value.

Here, the children gave no consideration for being named as beneficiaries. If Dana can establish an enforceable contract, equity would impose a constructive trust on the proceeds for her benefit, because the children's claim rests solely on a designation that Lee had contractually promised not to make.

## The Critical Question: Enforceability of the Oral Promise

The entire analysis turns on whether Dana can enforce the oral promise despite the Statute of Frauds. If she cannot, the children prevail straightforwardly under the last-filed beneficiary designation. If she can, she has a strong equitable claim to a constructive trust.

**Arguments favoring enforceability (Dana's position):**

1. **Full performance by the promisee.** Dana fully performed by marrying Lee. Many courts hold that full performance by one party takes an oral contract out of the Statute of Frauds, or at minimum triggers equitable estoppel against the promisor (or those claiming through the promisor) who would use the Statute as a shield for fraud.

2. **Part performance by the promisor.** Lee himself performed the promise by filing the beneficiary designation. This corroborates the existence and terms of the oral agreement and reduces concerns about fraudulent claims.

3. **Irreversible change of position.** Marriage is an irrevocable act—Dana cannot be restored to her pre-marriage status. Allowing the Statute of Frauds to defeat her claim would work a fraud upon her, which is precisely what equity seeks to prevent.

4. **Equitable estoppel.** The children are not parties to the insurance contract and gave no consideration. Allowing them to invoke the Statute of Frauds (a defense personal to the promisor) to defeat Dana's equitable claim may be impermissible.

**Arguments against enforceability (the children's position):**

1. **The Statute of Frauds is clear.** Agreements made in consideration of marriage must be in writing. The legislature enacted this requirement precisely because of the high stakes and emotional context surrounding marriage, making oral promises particularly unreliable.

2. **The part-performance doctrine varies by jurisdiction.** Not all jurisdictions recognize part performance as removing an oral agreement from the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds (as opposed to the real-property provision, where part performance is more widely accepted).

3. **Policy favoring free alienability of beneficiary designations.** Insurance law strongly favors the insured's right to change beneficiaries. Allowing oral promises to override formal beneficiary changes would create uncertainty and invite fraudulent claims by alleged promisees.

4. **The children stand in Lee's shoes for purposes of the by-laws.** The association's by-laws authorized the change, Lee followed proper procedures, and the children's rights derive from the association's contract with Lee, not from any wrongful act.

## The Better Claim

**The children likely have the better claim to the insurance proceeds**, though the question is close and jurisdiction-dependent.

The majority of courts addressing this precise scenario have held that an **oral** antenuptial promise to maintain a beneficiary designation is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds' marriage provision. While courts are sympathetic to the equitable arguments Dana can raise, the Statute of Frauds serves an important evidentiary function, and courts are reluctant to enforce oral promises—particularly after the promisor is dead and cannot testify about the terms or existence of the agreement. The parol evidence concerns are especially acute here because the only surviving party with knowledge of the alleged promise is the very person who stands to benefit from its enforcement.

Moreover, from the perspective of the fraternal association, the by-laws created a clear procedure for changing beneficiaries, and Lee followed it. The association is entitled to pay the proceeds to the last-named beneficiary of record, and courts generally uphold the regularity of such designations to preserve the administrability of insurance contracts.

That said, if Dana were in a jurisdiction that broadly applies equitable estoppel or the part-performance doctrine to the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds, she could prevail. Her argument is strongest if the court views the Statute of Frauds as a rule designed to prevent fraud and concludes that enforcing it here would *perpetrate* a fraud—Lee induced Dana into marriage with a promise he later repudiated, and his estate (through his children) should not benefit from that broken promise.

**In summary:** Under the prevailing legal rules, the children have the better claim because (1) the oral promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, (2) the by-laws authorized the beneficiary change, (3) Lee followed proper procedures, and (4) the last-filed designation controls. Dana's equitable arguments—constructive trust, estoppel, and part performance—are meritorious but face the substantial barrier of the Statute of Frauds and the strong policy favoring the insured's freedom to change beneficiaries. Were the promise in writing, Dana would almost certainly prevail through a constructive trust theory, since the children are mere volunteers. The absence of a writing is dispositive in most jurisdictions.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to the Insurance Proceeds

This dispute requires analyzing several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral prenuptial promise, the nature of beneficiary designations under fraternal insurance certificates, and the effect of a valid beneficiary change under the association's by-laws.

## Lee's Oral Prenuptial Promise

Lee's oral promise to Dana—that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate—has the hallmarks of a contract supported by consideration. Dana's agreement to marry Lee constitutes valid consideration for Lee's promise. Marriage as consideration for a contractual promise has long been recognized at law, and the fact that Dana actually performed (by marrying Lee) removes any issue of executory consideration.

The critical question is whether this oral promise is enforceable despite the absence of a writing. Under the Statute of Frauds, a contract made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee never signed a written agreement memorializing this promise. Thus, on its face, the Statute of Frauds would render Lee's oral promise unenforceable.

However, Dana has a strong argument for taking the case outside the Statute of Frauds under the doctrine of part performance or, more precisely, full performance. Dana fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee. Many jurisdictions hold that when one party has fully performed under an oral contract that would otherwise fall within the Statute of Frauds, the Statute does not bar enforcement, because allowing the promisor to invoke the Statute after receiving the full benefit of the bargain would work a fraud. Courts in equity have been particularly willing to enforce oral prenuptial agreements where the marriage has been performed, on the theory that the marriage itself constitutes an irreversible change of position that makes it unconscionable to permit the promisor to repudiate.

Moreover, Lee himself initially honored the promise by filing the standard form naming Dana as beneficiary. This act of partial performance by the promisor corroborates the existence of the oral agreement and further undermines any Statute of Frauds defense.

If the oral promise is enforceable, it created a contractual obligation on Lee's part to designate Dana as beneficiary and, by implication, to maintain that designation. The promise was not merely to make the initial designation but to confer on Dana the benefit of the insurance proceeds—the entire purpose of the promise being to induce the marriage.

## The Nature of the Beneficiary Designation and Lee's Power to Change It

Under the association's by-laws, a member retained the right to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. This is a standard reservation-of-right-to-change clause, and under ordinary insurance law principles, a beneficiary named under such a certificate has no vested interest in the proceeds—only an expectancy that can be defeated at any time by the insured's exercise of the power to change.

Lee exercised this power by filing a sworn request naming his two children as the new beneficiaries. If we look only at the insurance certificate and the association's by-laws, the children hold the valid designation at the time of Lee's death, and the association would be obligated to pay them.

## The Contractual Restriction on Lee's Power to Change the Beneficiary

The crucial analytical point is that although Lee had the **power** under the by-laws to change the beneficiary, he did not have the **right** to do so as against Dana, if the oral prenuptial promise is enforceable. There is a well-established distinction in insurance law between the power to change a beneficiary (which derives from the contract with the insurer and the insurer's rules) and the right to change a beneficiary (which may be constrained by a separate contractual obligation to a third party).

When an insured has contractually obligated himself to maintain a particular beneficiary designation, the insured's subsequent change of beneficiary, while technically effective as between the insured and the insurer, constitutes a breach of the contract with the original beneficiary. Courts in equity will impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in favor of the party to whom the contractual obligation was owed.

This is precisely the framework that applies here. Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage and rendered enforceable by Dana's full performance, created an equitable right in Dana to remain as beneficiary. Lee's later change of beneficiary to his children was a breach of that contractual obligation. While the change may have been procedurally valid under the association's rules—meaning the association itself could pay the children and be discharged—equity will not allow the children to retain the proceeds as against Dana's superior equitable claim.

## The Children's Position

The children are volunteer beneficiaries. They gave no consideration for their designation. They are, in effect, donee beneficiaries of Lee's later designation. A donee beneficiary's claim is subordinate to the equitable claim of a party who holds a contractual right to the proceeds supported by consideration. The children can claim no greater right than Lee himself could have asserted, and Lee could not have defeated Dana's contractual right by his unilateral act of changing the beneficiary.

The children might argue that they are bona fide beneficiaries who relied on the designation, but mere designation as a beneficiary does not create detrimental reliance of the sort that would defeat a prior equitable interest, particularly when the children provided no consideration.

## The Equitable Remedy

The appropriate remedy is the imposition of a constructive trust. A court of equity would order that the insurance proceeds, even if paid to the children (or into court), be held in constructive trust for Dana's benefit. The constructive trust arises because Lee's change of beneficiary, in violation of his contractual obligation to Dana, constituted an unjust enrichment of the children at Dana's expense.

## Conclusion

**Dana has the better claim to the insurance proceeds.** Although the Statute of Frauds poses an obstacle to enforcement of Lee's oral prenuptial promise, Dana's full performance of her side of the bargain—by entering into the marriage—takes the agreement outside the Statute or, at minimum, provides a basis for equitable enforcement. The oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage and corroborated by Lee's initial act of designating Dana as beneficiary, created a contractual right in Dana that Lee could not unilaterally defeat by exercising his by-law power to change the beneficiary. The children, as volunteer beneficiaries who gave no consideration, take subject to Dana's equitable interest. A court should impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in Dana's favor, as Lee's change of beneficiary was a breach of his contractual obligation and the children would be unjustly enriched if permitted to retain the proceeds.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to Insurance Proceeds

This problem requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the legal effect of the beneficiary designation and subsequent change, and the interplay between contract law, insurance law, and equitable principles.

## Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Lee orally promised Dana that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana's marriage to Lee constitutes valid consideration for that promise—she changed her legal position by entering into the marriage in reliance on Lee's undertaking. This creates a contract supported by consideration, not a mere gratuitous promise.

However, two significant obstacles confront Dana's attempt to enforce this promise as an irrevocable commitment:

**The Statute of Frauds.** In many jurisdictions, agreements made in consideration of marriage fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee never signed a written agreement memorializing his promise. Dana would argue that Lee's subsequent act of filing the beneficiary designation form constitutes part performance or satisfies the writing requirement. The beneficiary designation form, however, is a standard insurance document—not a contract between Lee and Dana—and likely does not satisfy the Statute of Frauds because it was not intended to memorialize the parties' agreement and may not contain the essential terms of their bargain (i.e., that the designation was irrevocable and made in exchange for marriage). Most courts would find the Statute of Frauds bars enforcement of the oral promise standing alone.

**Part Performance and Estoppel.** Dana might invoke the doctrine of part performance or equitable estoppel to overcome the Statute of Frauds. She fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee. Courts are split on whether marriage alone constitutes sufficient part performance. Some courts have held that where one party has fully performed an oral agreement made in consideration of marriage, the Statute of Frauds will not bar enforcement, particularly where the performing party cannot be restored to the status quo ante (a marriage having been entered into). Other courts are more reluctant, requiring additional indicia of reliance beyond the marriage itself. Dana's argument is strengthened by the fact that Lee actually followed through initially by naming her as beneficiary, corroborating the existence of the agreement.

## The Beneficiary Designation and the Right to Change

Even assuming Dana can overcome the Statute of Frauds, a critical question remains: did Lee's promise create an **irrevocable** beneficiary designation, or did Dana remain a revocable beneficiary subject to change under the association's by-laws?

**General Rule on Beneficiary Changes.** Under standard insurance law principles, the insured-member retains the right to change beneficiaries unless that right has been contractually surrendered. The association's by-laws expressly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee followed the prescribed procedure. If Dana is treated as an ordinary revocable beneficiary, Lee's later designation of his children effectively superseded her designation, and the children would prevail.

**Dana's Argument for Irrevocability.** Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, created a **separate contract** between Lee and Dana—independent of the insurance certificate itself—under which Lee was obligated to maintain Dana as beneficiary. Under this theory, even though the association's by-laws permitted beneficiary changes, Lee had contractually bound himself *vis-à-vis Dana* not to exercise that right. The distinction is between (1) Lee's power to change the beneficiary under the insurance contract (which the association's rules granted him), and (2) Lee's contractual duty to Dana not to exercise that power (arising from their separate agreement).

Courts have recognized this distinction. A beneficiary who has given consideration for the designation—sometimes called a "beneficiary for value"—acquires an equitable interest that the insured cannot defeat by a unilateral change. The classic formulation is that where a beneficiary designation is supported by a valid contract and valuable consideration, the insured holds the policy (or certificate) in constructive trust for the beneficiary, and a subsequent change is ineffective as against the beneficiary's equitable rights.

## The Decisive Question: Can Dana Prove and Enforce the Underlying Contract?

The analysis thus turns on whether Dana can establish an enforceable contract. If she can, equity will impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in her favor despite the formal beneficiary change. If she cannot—primarily because of the Statute of Frauds—then the children, as the properly designated beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death, take the proceeds.

**Arguments Favoring Dana:**

1. *Consideration existed.* Marriage is classic, valuable consideration. The promise was not illusory or gratuitous.

2. *Full performance.* Dana fully performed by marrying Lee. Equity traditionally disfavors allowing a party who has received the benefit of full performance to invoke the Statute of Frauds to avoid reciprocal obligations. The doctrine of part (or full) performance is particularly compelling here because Dana cannot be "unmarried"—there is no adequate remedy at law that restores her to the status quo ante.

3. *Corroboration.* Lee's subsequent act of actually naming Dana as beneficiary corroborates the existence of the oral agreement and negates any suggestion of fabrication—one of the primary concerns the Statute of Frauds is designed to address.

4. *Equitable estoppel.* Lee induced Dana to change her position (by marrying him) in reliance on his promise. Allowing his estate or his children to benefit from his breach would unjustly enrich them at Dana's expense.

**Arguments Favoring the Children:**

1. *Statute of Frauds.* The promise was oral, made in consideration of marriage, and never reduced to a signed writing. The statute exists precisely to prevent fraudulent or uncertain claims in this context, and courts should enforce it.

2. *The by-laws control.* The insurance certificate is a contract between Lee and the association. The by-laws expressly reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries. Lee followed the proper procedure. The association is entitled to rely on its own records and procedures, and the children, as the last-named beneficiaries, have a superior claim under the terms of the certificate.

3. *Policy concerns.* Allowing oral promises to override formal beneficiary designations would undermine the certainty and reliability of insurance beneficiary systems. Insurance associations need clear rules about to whom they must pay proceeds.

4. *Dana's claim is against Lee's estate, not the proceeds.* Even if Lee breached a contract with Dana, the children might argue that Dana's remedy is a breach-of-contract action against Lee's estate for damages, not an equitable claim to the insurance proceeds themselves.

## The Weight of Authority and Likely Outcome

The majority of courts that have addressed this issue have held that **where a beneficiary designation is made pursuant to a contract supported by valuable consideration, the beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest that the insured cannot defeat by a subsequent change.** This principle has been applied in cases involving marriage consideration specifically.

However, the Statute of Frauds remains a formidable barrier. In jurisdictions that strictly apply the Statute of Frauds to agreements made in consideration of marriage, Dana's claim will fail unless she can invoke part performance or estoppel. The trend in modern courts, influenced by the Restatement (Second) of Contracts and equitable principles, is to recognize that **full performance by one party removes the contract from the Statute of Frauds** or, alternatively, that **equitable estoppel prevents the other party (or those claiming through him) from raising the statute as a defense.**

If the court finds the oral contract enforceable—either because full performance takes it outside the Statute of Frauds or because estoppel applies—then Dana prevails. The court would impose a **constructive trust** on the insurance proceeds in Dana's favor, reasoning that Lee held the power to change beneficiaries subject to a contractual duty not to do so, and his breach of that duty cannot defeat Dana's equitable interest. The children, as volunteers (they gave no consideration for their designation), would take subject to Dana's prior equitable claim.

If the court strictly enforces the Statute of Frauds and refuses to apply part performance or estoppel, the **children prevail** because they are the properly designated beneficiaries under the certificate at the time of Lee's death, and the oral promise is unenforceable.

## Conclusion

**Dana has the better claim in most jurisdictions**, but the outcome is not certain and depends heavily on the jurisdiction's treatment of the Statute of Frauds. The strongest analysis proceeds as follows: Lee's promise, supported by the consideration of Dana's marriage, created an enforceable contract. Dana's full performance (marriage) takes the agreement outside the Statute of Frauds or, alternatively, estops Lee's successors from invoking the statute. As a beneficiary for value, Dana acquired a vested equitable interest in the proceeds that Lee could not unilaterally defeat. Lee's subsequent beneficiary change was a breach of his contractual obligation to Dana, and equity will impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in her favor. The children, who gave no consideration and are mere volunteers, take nothing.

The children's best hope lies in a jurisdiction that rigidly applies the Statute of Frauds to agreements in consideration of marriage and refuses to recognize full performance or estoppel exceptions—but this represents the minority position in modern American law.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem raises intersecting issues of contract law, insurance law, and the enforceability of promises regarding beneficiary designations. The central question is whether Dana can enforce Lee's pre-marriage oral promise to make her the beneficiary, or whether the children's claim under the later, formally executed beneficiary change prevails.

## Dana's Potential Theories

**1. Breach of Contract Based on the Oral Promise**

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—was a contract supported by valid consideration. Marriage is classic and well-recognized consideration for a promise. Dana performed by marrying Lee, and Lee initially performed by filing the beneficiary designation naming her. Dana would argue that the promise implicitly included an obligation not to later change the beneficiary, or at least that the promise created a vested contractual right in her that Lee could not unilaterally revoke.

However, this theory faces several significant obstacles:

*Statute of Frauds.* In many jurisdictions, a promise made in consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. The problem states that no written agreement memorializing the promise was ever signed by Lee. Under the traditional Statute of Frauds provision covering promises made in consideration of marriage (distinct from the mutual promises to marry, which are excluded), Dana's claim based on the oral promise would be unenforceable unless an exception applies.

Dana might argue that the Statute of Frauds is satisfied by part performance or estoppel. She did marry Lee (full performance of her side of the bargain), and Lee did initially designate her as beneficiary. Some courts hold that full performance by one party takes an oral contract outside the Statute of Frauds, reasoning that the equities demand enforcement when the promisee has fully performed and cannot be restored to the status quo ante. Marriage, once performed, obviously cannot be "undone" as a practical matter. This is a plausible argument, but courts are not uniform on whether the marriage-provision exception can be overcome by performance alone, and some jurisdictions strictly enforce the writing requirement.

*Parol Evidence and Interpretation.* Even if the oral promise is enforceable despite the Statute of Frauds, there is a question about its scope. Did Lee promise to make Dana the beneficiary permanently and irrevocably, or merely to designate her at that time? The promise as stated—"he would make her the beneficiary"—is arguably ambiguous. Lee did make her the beneficiary. He could contend (were he alive) that the promise was fully performed when the designation was filed, and that it did not include a promise never to change the designation. Dana would need to establish that the promise encompassed an irrevocable commitment.

**2. Constructive Trust or Equitable Estoppel**

Even if the oral contract is unenforceable at law, Dana might seek equitable relief. Courts have sometimes imposed constructive trusts on insurance proceeds where a beneficiary change was procured in violation of a contractual obligation, on the theory that allowing the new beneficiaries to take the proceeds would unjustly enrich them. Under this theory, the children would hold the proceeds as constructive trustees for Dana.

However, constructive trust is an equitable remedy typically applied when there has been fraud, breach of a fiduciary duty, or unjust enrichment traceable to wrongful conduct. The children are innocent third parties—Lee's own children from a prior marriage—and they did nothing wrongful. The question is whether Lee's breach of his alleged promise to Dana is sufficient wrongful conduct to warrant the imposition of a constructive trust. Courts have reached different conclusions, but many require a clear and enforceable underlying obligation before imposing such a trust.

## The Children's Claim

**1. The Association's By-Laws Control**

The children's strongest argument is grounded in insurance law principles. The insurance certificate was issued by a fraternal benefit association, and the association's by-laws expressly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed the prescribed procedure exactly. Under well-established insurance law, the insured member retains the right to change beneficiaries unless that right has been contractually surrendered in a manner recognized by the insurance contract or by-laws. A named beneficiary under a policy that reserves the right to change beneficiaries has a mere expectancy, not a vested right.

The by-laws here allowed changes, and Lee exercised that right through proper channels. The association is obligated to pay according to the last valid beneficiary designation on file, which names the children.

**2. The Oral Promise Does Not Bind the Association or Override the By-Laws**

The fraternal association was not a party to Lee's oral promise to Dana. Insurance associations pay according to their governing documents. Even if Lee breached a private agreement with Dana, that breach does not invalidate the beneficiary change as between Lee and the association. The association's records show the children as the designated beneficiaries, and the association would be entitled—indeed obligated—to pay accordingly.

**3. Statute of Frauds Defense**

The children, as third-party beneficiaries of the insurance proceeds, can raise the Statute of Frauds as a defense to Dana's contract claim. Since no writing memorializes Lee's promise, and the promise was made in consideration of marriage, the children would argue the promise is unenforceable.

**4. No Consideration Running to the Children / Innocent Takers**

The children gave no consideration and made no promise; they are simply the last-named beneficiaries under a validly executed change. They stand on the formal legal documentation.

## The Likely Outcome

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds, for several reinforcing reasons:

First, Lee's oral promise to Dana is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as a promise made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to a signed writing. While Dana's full performance (the marriage) creates an argument for taking the promise outside the statute, this argument is uncertain and jurisdiction-dependent, and many courts would decline to enforce the oral promise.

Second, even if the oral promise were somehow enforceable, its terms are ambiguous as to whether Lee promised permanent, irrevocable beneficiary status or merely promised to designate Dana at the time. The more natural reading is that Lee promised to name her as beneficiary—which he did—not that he promised never to exercise his contractual right under the by-laws to make a change.

Third, and most fundamentally, insurance law strongly favors honoring the last properly executed beneficiary designation. The association's by-laws expressly reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries, and he exercised that right through the prescribed procedure. A beneficiary under a revocable designation has no vested interest in the policy proceeds—only an expectancy that can be defeated at any time. Dana's designation was revocable, and it was revoked.

Fourth, even under an equitable theory, courts are generally reluctant to impose constructive trusts that override formal insurance beneficiary designations absent clear evidence of fraud, undue influence, or a written agreement restricting the right to change beneficiaries. An unwritten, arguably ambiguous oral promise—even one supported by the consideration of marriage—is typically insufficient.

Dana's remedy, if any, would lie in a breach of contract action against Lee's estate (assuming the Statute of Frauds could be overcome), not in a direct claim to the insurance proceeds themselves. But even that claim is weakened by the Statute of Frauds problem and the ambiguity of the promise. The insurance proceeds themselves should be paid to the children as the last validly designated beneficiaries under the association's governing documents.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to the Insurance Proceeds

This dispute requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the effect of the original beneficiary designation naming Dana, the member's power to change beneficiaries under the association's by-laws, and whether any equitable doctrine prevents the change from taking effect.

## Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's strongest theoretical argument rests on the idea that Lee's oral promise to name her as beneficiary, made in consideration of marriage, created an enforceable contract that should override the later change. However, this argument faces a formidable obstacle: the Statute of Frauds. Under the traditional Statute of Frauds—and as codified in virtually every jurisdiction—a promise made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee never signed any written agreement memorializing the promise to make Dana an irrevocable beneficiary. The oral promise is therefore unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

Dana might argue part performance as an exception. She did marry Lee (the requested consideration), and Lee did in fact designate her as beneficiary after the marriage, arguably acknowledging the obligation. Courts have recognized part performance as taking an agreement outside the Statute of Frauds in some contexts, particularly in real-property cases where the elements of possession, payment, and improvements are present. However, the part-performance doctrine has historically been applied more narrowly to promises in consideration of marriage. Marriage alone, as the performance of the consideration, has generally not been deemed sufficient part performance to overcome the Statute of Frauds for this category of promise, because marriage is the very act that brings the promise within the statute in the first place. Allowing marriage itself to constitute the part performance would effectively nullify the statute's requirement for this entire category.

## The Beneficiary Designation and Power to Change

Even setting aside the Statute of Frauds problem, Dana faces a fundamental structural issue rooted in insurance and fraternal-benefit-association law. The association's by-laws expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee complied with that procedure when he designated his children. Under well-established principles, a named beneficiary in a life-insurance certificate—particularly one issued by a fraternal benefit association—has no vested right in the proceeds during the insured's lifetime when the certificate or governing by-laws reserve the right to change beneficiaries. The beneficiary's interest is a mere expectancy, not a vested property right.

The original designation of Dana was therefore revocable on its face. When Lee followed the association's prescribed procedure and filed a sworn request naming his children, the change was effective under the contract between Lee and the association (the certificate as governed by the by-laws). Dana's designation was superseded.

## Could Dana's Claim Be Treated as Creating an Irrevocable Beneficiary Designation?

Dana's most promising argument is that Lee's promise, coupled with the valuable consideration of marriage, converted what would otherwise be a revocable designation into an irrevocable one—essentially that a contract existed between Lee and Dana that equity should enforce against the children. Some courts have recognized that when an insured, for valuable consideration, promises a third party to name and maintain that person as beneficiary, the insured may be equitably estopped from changing the designation, or a constructive trust may be imposed on the proceeds in favor of the promisee.

However, for this doctrine to apply, most courts require:

1. **A valid and enforceable agreement** supported by consideration to maintain the beneficiary designation. Here, the oral promise fails the Statute of Frauds, so there is no enforceable agreement in the first place.

2. **Clear and convincing evidence** of the agreement's terms, particularly that the designation was to be irrevocable. Lee promised to "make" Dana the beneficiary—this language speaks to the initial designation but does not clearly promise never to change it. Courts distinguish between a promise to name someone as beneficiary (which may be satisfied by the initial designation, after which the power to change remains) and a promise to maintain someone as an irrevocable beneficiary. The facts suggest only the former.

3. **Compliance with applicable formalities.** Even where equity might intervene, the absence of a writing signed by Lee undermines Dana's position.

## The Children's Position

The children hold the stronger claim. They were designated as beneficiaries through the procedure authorized by the association's by-laws. The association's contract with Lee (the certificate governed by the by-laws) expressly contemplated changes in beneficiary, and Lee complied with the required formalities. The children's designation was the last effective beneficiary change on file at Lee's death.

From the association's perspective, it is obligated to pay the proceeds to the last properly designated beneficiaries. The children satisfy that requirement. Dana does not, because her designation was formally superseded.

## Equitable Arguments and Their Limitations

Dana might invoke equitable estoppel or seek imposition of a constructive trust, arguing that Lee's change of beneficiary constituted a fraud on her marital expectations. However, equitable relief generally requires an underlying enforceable obligation. Because the Statute of Frauds renders the oral promise unenforceable, and because the promise as described does not clearly establish irrevocability, equity has no enforceable obligation to protect. Courts are reluctant to use equity to circumvent the Statute of Frauds absent extraordinary circumstances such as fraud (beyond mere non-performance of an oral promise), and the facts do not indicate fraud in the traditional sense—Lee simply exercised a contractual right reserved to him under the certificate.

Furthermore, even if equity were inclined to intervene, the children are donee beneficiaries who gave no consideration for their designation. While this means they are not bona fide purchasers for value, their status as donees does not automatically subordinate them to Dana's claim when Dana herself lacks an enforceable legal or equitable right.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was made in consideration of marriage and was never reduced to a writing signed by Lee. Even apart from the Statute of Frauds, the association's by-laws reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries, making Dana's designation revocable. Lee exercised that right in compliance with the prescribed procedure. Dana's expectancy was extinguished when the new designation took effect. No equitable doctrine—estoppel, constructive trust, or otherwise—is likely to overcome these barriers, because there is no enforceable underlying obligation to which equity can attach. The association should pay the proceeds to Lee's children as the last properly designated beneficiaries.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to Insurance Proceeds

This problem requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the effect of his initial designation of Dana as beneficiary, the validity of his subsequent beneficiary change, and whether Dana has any legal theory that would override the later designation.

## Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Lee orally promised Dana before marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana provided consideration by marrying Lee, and Lee performed by filing the designation form. The question is whether this oral promise creates an irrevocable right in Dana that survives Lee's later change of beneficiary.

**Statute of Frauds Concerns.** In many jurisdictions, agreements made in consideration of marriage fall within the Statute of Frauds and must be in writing to be enforceable. Lee's promise—"if you marry me, I will make you my beneficiary"—is precisely the kind of agreement the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds targets. No written agreement memorializing the promise was ever signed by Lee. Dana would argue part performance or estoppel (she did marry him, and he did initially designate her), but the traditional rule is strict: an oral agreement made in consideration of marriage is unenforceable. This significantly undermines Dana's contract-based claim.

Even if a court were to find the Statute of Frauds satisfied through full performance (Lee did designate her, and Dana did marry him), Dana faces additional obstacles.

## The Nature of the Beneficiary Designation and the Association's By-Laws

Fraternal benefit associations are governed by their charters, constitutions, and by-laws, which form the contract between the member and the association. Here, the by-laws expressly allowed a member to change beneficiaries at any time by filing a sworn request. Lee followed this procedure when he named his two children as the new beneficiaries.

The general rule governing fraternal benefit certificates is that **the member retains the right to change the beneficiary at any time in accordance with the association's rules, and a named beneficiary has no vested interest in the proceeds—only an expectancy**. This principle has been consistently upheld. The beneficiary designation is revocable unless the member has contractually surrendered the power to change it in a manner recognized by the association.

## Can Dana's Oral Promise Override the By-Laws?

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, created a binding contract that effectively made her designation irrevocable. Under this theory, even though Lee had the *power* to change the beneficiary under the association's rules, he lacked the *right* to do so because he had contractually bound himself to maintain Dana as beneficiary.

Courts have recognized that a **separate, enforceable contract** between the insured and the beneficiary can create an equitable right in the beneficiary that survives a later change. Under this theory, even if the legal designation now names the children, Dana could seek to impose a **constructive trust** on the proceeds in her favor, arguing that Lee breached his contractual obligation by changing the beneficiary.

However, this theory requires an **enforceable** contract, and Dana's claim fails on multiple grounds:

1. **Statute of Frauds.** As noted, the oral promise made in consideration of marriage is unenforceable absent a writing signed by Lee. The standard beneficiary designation form filed with the association is not a memorandum of the pre-marriage agreement—it is merely the association's administrative form and does not reference any contractual obligation to maintain Dana as beneficiary.

2. **Association By-Laws Control.** Many jurisdictions hold that the by-laws of a fraternal association are part of the insurance contract and that members cannot, by private side agreements, alter the rights established by those by-laws—particularly the right to change beneficiaries.
