However, a well-established exception exists in equity: when an insured designates a beneficiary pursuant to a valid contract supported by valuable consideration, the beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest in the policy proceeds. Marriage is recognized in law as valuable consideration. Because Dana gave valuable consideration in exchange for the designation, her interest in the policy vested immediately upon Lee naming her.

While the fraternal association’s bylaws permitted Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, this rule primarily governs the administrative relationship between the association and the member to protect the association from multiple claims. It does not defeat the vested equitable rights of a beneficiary who acquired that status as a purchaser for value. By contracting away his right to change the beneficiary in exchange for marriage, Lee surrendered his equitable authority to freely designate a new beneficiary. 

When Lee subsequently named his children as the new beneficiaries, he breached his fully executed agreement with Dana. Because the children were named as mere gratuitous donees (having provided no consideration for the designation), their claim is subordinate to Dana’s vested equitable interest. Therefore, equity will treat the proceeds as belonging to Dana, giving her the superior claim.

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. The outcome of this dispute depends on whether Lee’s oral pre-marriage promise created a vested equitable right in the insurance proceeds that stripped him of his contractual right to change the beneficiary. Because the oral agreement is unenforceable, Dana’s claim fails, and the subsequent valid designation of the children controls.

First, Lee’s oral promise to Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. A promise made in consideration of marriage—other than mutual promises to marry—falls squarely within the Statute of Frauds and must be memorialized in writing to be legally binding. Lee promised to name Dana as his beneficiary specifically in exchange for her marrying him. Because no written agreement was ever signed, the underlying contract is presumptively unenforceable. 

Second, no exceptions to the Statute of Frauds apply to save Dana’s claim. It is a well-established legal principle that marriage alone does not constitute sufficient part performance to take an oral agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. Furthermore, Lee’s subsequent act of filing the association’s standard beneficiary form does not satisfy the writing requirement. To satisfy the Statute of Frauds, a writing must contain the essential terms of the contract. A standard life-insurance beneficiary form merely identifies the recipient of the proceeds upon death; it does not reference the premarital promise, nor does it state that the designation is irrevocable or supported by consideration. 

Even if Dana were to argue that Lee’s act of naming her as beneficiary constituted full performance of the oral contract, this argument fails to secure her the proceeds. If filing the standard form was the full extent of his promise, he fully performed by giving her a revocable expectancy, which he later lawfully revoked. If his promise was to make her the irrevocable beneficiary, he never fully performed that promise, leaving the agreement subject to the Statute of Frauds. 

Because the oral contract is unenforceable, Dana acquired no vested legal or equitable interest in the life-insurance certificate. In the context of fraternal insurance associations and life insurance generally, when the governing by-laws or the policy allow the insured to change the beneficiary, the designated beneficiary holds a mere expectancy during the insured's lifetime, not a vested right. The association’s by-laws expressly permitted Lee to change his beneficiary at any time by filing a sworn request. 

Since Dana had no legally enforceable contract to restrict Lee’s actions, Lee retained his absolute right as a member of the association to change his beneficiary. He successfully exercised this right by following the association's required procedure, filing a sworn request to designate his two children as the new beneficiaries. 

Ultimately, Dana’s claim rests on an unenforceable oral promise and a revoked expectancy. The children’s claim rests on a valid, legally executed change of beneficiary that strictly complied with the association’s by-laws. Therefore, the children have the superior legal claim and are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim to the life insurance proceeds because the oral agreement between Lee and Dana is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, leaving Lee free to exercise his right to change the beneficiary. 

At the heart of this dispute is whether Dana acquired a vested equitable interest in the life insurance certificate that would prevent Lee from later changing the beneficiary. Generally, the beneficiary of a life insurance policy—particularly one issued by a fraternal benefit association whose by-laws expressly allow for the changing of beneficiaries—has only a mere expectancy, not a vested right, in the proceeds during the insured's lifetime. An insured may freely change the beneficiary unless the original beneficiary has acquired a vested interest through a valid, enforceable contract. 

Dana’s claim relies on the pre-marriage agreement, but this agreement violates the Statute of Frauds. Under the Statute of Frauds, any agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Lee’s promise to name Dana as his beneficiary was made strictly in exchange for her marrying him. Because this promise was entirely oral, it is legally unenforceable. 

Dana cannot successfully argue that her act of marrying Lee constituted part performance sufficient to take the oral contract out of the Statute of Frauds. Courts uniformly hold that marriage alone is not sufficient part performance to bypass the Statute of Frauds; if it were, the specific provision requiring contracts in consideration of marriage to be in writing would be rendered entirely meaningless. 

Furthermore, Lee’s subsequent act of filing the association’s standard form naming Dana as the beneficiary did not satisfy the Statute of Frauds, nor did it constitute full performance of an irrevocable contract. To serve as a sufficient written memorandum under the Statute of Frauds, a writing must contain the essential terms of the agreement, including the consideration. A standard beneficiary designation form merely directs the association on who to pay upon death; it does not reference the prenuptial promise or state that the designation was made in consideration of marriage. Additionally, because the association's by-laws made the designation revocable, Lee's act of naming her did not constitute full performance of a promise to permanently surrender his right to change the beneficiary. 

Because the oral contract was unenforceable, Dana never acquired a vested, irrevocable right to the insurance proceeds. She held only a revocable expectancy. Lee retained his absolute right under the association’s by-laws to change his beneficiary. Since he properly followed the association's procedures by filing a sworn request to name his children as the new beneficiaries, that subsequent designation is legally valid and binding. Therefore, the children are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

Dana has the better claim to the insurance proceeds because she acquired a vested equitable interest in the policy that Lee could not unilaterally revoke. The resolution of this dispute depends on the intersection of the Statute of Frauds, the doctrine of full performance, and the equitable rights of life-insurance beneficiaries.

Initially, Lee’s oral promise to make Dana the beneficiary of his life insurance certificate in exchange for her marrying him falls squarely within the Statute of Frauds. Under the Statute of Frauds, any agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing to be legally enforceable. Because Lee never signed a written agreement memorializing the promise, the contract was initially unenforceable. Furthermore, it is a well-established legal principle that marriage alone does not constitute sufficient part performance to take an oral prenuptial agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. 

However, the Statute of Frauds does not apply to contracts that have been fully executed. After the marriage, Lee followed through on his oral promise by filing the association’s standard form and officially naming Dana as the beneficiary of his certificate. By marrying Lee, Dana fully performed her side of the bargain; by designating Dana as the beneficiary, Lee fully performed his. This complete performance by both parties removed the oral agreement from the operation of the Statute of Frauds, rendering the underlying contract valid and binding. 

Because the contract was validly executed, Dana’s legal status under the insurance certificate transformed. In standard life insurance policies and fraternal benefit associations, a designated beneficiary typically possesses a mere "expectancy" rather than a vested right, meaning the insured is free to change the beneficiary at any time. However, an exception exists in equity: when an insured promises to designate an individual as a beneficiary in exchange for valuable consideration (such as marriage), and subsequently fulfills that promise, the beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest in the policy proceeds. 

The fraternal association’s by-laws allowing a member to change beneficiaries via a sworn request do not alter Dana’s vested rights. Such by-laws and standard forms are designed to protect the insurer from multiple liability and to provide a clear administrative procedure for the association. They do not govern or defeat the underlying equitable rights and contractual obligations between the insured and third parties. By contracting with Dana for valuable consideration, Lee effectively waived his equitable right to exercise the association's change-of-beneficiary provision to her detriment. 

When Lee later filed a sworn request to name his two children from an earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries, he breached his fully executed contract with Dana. Because the children are gratuitous donees—meaning they provided no consideration for the beneficiary designation—they stand in Lee’s shoes and take their interest subject to Dana’s prior vested equitable rights. Since Dana holds a superior equitable interest supported by valuable consideration, her claim overrides the subsequent, gratuitous designation of Lee’s children. Therefore, Dana is entitled to the insurance proceeds.

Dana has the better claim to the life insurance proceeds. 

The first issue is whether Lee’s oral promise to name Dana as his beneficiary in exchange for her marrying him is barred by the Statute of Frauds. Under the Statute of Frauds, an agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged to be enforceable. Furthermore, courts uniformly hold that the act of marriage itself does not constitute sufficient part performance to remove an oral agreement from the Statute of Frauds. Therefore, at the exact time of the wedding, Dana could not have legally enforced Lee’s oral promise.

However, the Statute of Frauds does not render an oral contract void, but merely unenforceable, and full performance of the contract by the promisor removes it from the statute's restrictions. After the marriage, Lee fulfilled his oral promise by filing the association’s standard form and officially naming Dana as the beneficiary. By executing the beneficiary designation, Lee fully performed his obligation under the oral agreement. This post-marriage performance validated the contract, rendering the Statute of Frauds inapplicable and effectuating a binding agreement between Lee and Dana supported by the consideration of her marriage to him.

The subsequent issue is whether Lee retained the right to change the beneficiary to his children, given that the association's by-laws expressly allowed members to change beneficiaries via a sworn request. As a general rule, when an insurance policy or a fraternal association's by-laws permit the insured to change the beneficiary, the designated beneficiary possesses only a mere expectancy, and the insured may change the designation at will. However, a well-established equitable exception applies when a beneficiary is designated pursuant to a valid contract supported by consideration. In such instances, the designated beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest in the policy and its proceeds. 

Because Lee named Dana as the beneficiary pursuant to their fully performed contract, Dana's interest in the certificate transformed from a mere revocable expectancy into a vested equitable right. Once Dana acquired this vested interest, Lee effectively contracted away his right to change the beneficiary. The association's by-laws governing the procedure for changing beneficiaries do not override a contractual, vested equitable interest held by a third party. 

When Lee later filed a sworn request to name his two children as the new beneficiaries, he breached his contractual duty to Dana. The children, who provided no consideration for the beneficiary change, are merely gratuitous donees. In property and insurance law, a beneficiary with a prior vested equitable interest prevails over subsequent volunteer donees. Because Dana holds a superior, vested equitable right to the certificate's proceeds, her claim supersedes the children's claim, and she is entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. The dispute centers on whether Lee’s oral promise to Dana created a vested equitable interest in the life-insurance certificate that precluded him from later changing the beneficiary. 

Under the Statute of Frauds, any agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged to be enforceable. Lee’s promise to make Dana the beneficiary of his life insurance was made expressly in consideration of her marrying him. Because no written agreement memorializing this promise was ever signed, the oral contract is unenforceable. 

Dana cannot successfully rely on standard exceptions to the Statute of Frauds to enforce the oral agreement. First, under the doctrine of part performance, the act of marriage itself is uniformly held to be insufficient to take an oral prenuptial agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. If marriage alone were enough, the specific Statute of Frauds provision requiring a writing for agreements in consideration of marriage would be rendered entirely meaningless. Second, Lee’s subsequent act of filing the association’s standard form naming Dana as the beneficiary does not constitute a sufficient written memorandum to satisfy the Statute of Frauds. To satisfy the writing requirement, a document must contain the essential terms of the agreement. A standard beneficiary designation form merely instructs the association on whom to pay upon death; it does not set forth the terms of the pre-marital contract or indicate that the designation was irrevocable and made in exchange for Dana’s promise to marry him.

Because the oral agreement is unenforceable, Dana did not acquire a vested equitable interest in the life-insurance certificate. In the absence of a valid, binding contract to the contrary, the designated beneficiary of a life-insurance policy—particularly one issued by a fraternal benefit association—possesses a mere expectancy rather than a vested legal right during the insured's lifetime. The insured retains the absolute right to change the beneficiary at will, provided they comply with the rules and procedures established by the insurer. 

Here, the association’s by-laws expressly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee strictly complied with this procedure when he subsequently designated his two children from an earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries. Because Dana had only a revocable expectancy that was lawfully extinguished by Lee's subsequent modification, Lee's final designation controls. Therefore, the children are the valid, recognized beneficiaries and possess the superior claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana has the better claim to the insurance proceeds. 

Under the Statute of Frauds, an agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing to be enforceable. Because Lee’s pre-marriage promise to make Dana the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate was made in exchange for her marrying him, the oral agreement fell squarely within the Statute of Frauds and was initially unenforceable. Furthermore, courts generally hold that the act of marriage alone does not constitute sufficient part performance to remove an oral antenuptial agreement from the Statute of Frauds.

However, the oral agreement became fully executed and legally binding after the wedding when Lee actually filed the association’s standard form naming Dana as the beneficiary. When both parties to an oral contract completely perform their respective obligations—Dana by marrying Lee, and Lee by officially designating her as the beneficiary—the agreement is taken out of the Statute of Frauds. Because the contract was fully executed, the lack of a written memorialization no longer barred its enforcement.

Once the agreement was executed, Dana acquired a vested equitable interest in the insurance proceeds. As a general rule, a beneficiary named in a life-insurance certificate (particularly one issued by a fraternal association) possesses a mere expectancy, and the insured retains the right to change the beneficiary at will if the policy or by-laws allow it. But this general rule only applies when the designation is gratuitous. When a member designates a beneficiary pursuant to a valid contract supported by valuable consideration—in this case, marriage—the beneficiary’s interest transforms from a mere expectancy into a vested equitable right. 

Because Dana held a vested equitable interest in the certificate, Lee contracted away his right to change the beneficiary to someone else. Although Lee later followed the association’s procedural by-laws to swear in his two children as the new beneficiaries, this subsequent change was ineffective against Dana. The children from his earlier marriage are mere volunteers (donees) who provided no consideration for their designation. Under the law, a prior vested equitable interest prevails over the competing claims of subsequent volunteers. Therefore, Dana’s vested contractual rights override Lee's later revocable designation, giving her the superior claim to the insurance proceeds.

Dana has the better claim to the life insurance proceeds. Although the initial oral promise was subject to the Statute of Frauds, Lee’s subsequent act of naming Dana as the beneficiary fully executed the contract. This granted Dana a vested equitable interest in the proceeds that precluded Lee from later changing the beneficiary.

Under the Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage is unenforceable unless it is memorialized in a signed writing. Here, Lee orally promised to name Dana as his life insurance beneficiary in exchange for her marrying him. Because Lee never signed a written agreement detailing this promise, the contract was initially unenforceable. Furthermore, under traditional contract principles, the act of marriage alone does not constitute sufficient part performance to take an oral antenuptial agreement out of the Statute of Frauds. 

However, an oral contract is removed from the Statute of Frauds and becomes fully enforceable when it has been fully performed (executed) by both parties. Dana fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee. Lee fully performed his side of the agreement when, after the wedding, he filed the association’s standard form officially designating Dana as the beneficiary. Although the standard beneficiary form did not contain the essential terms of the premarital agreement and therefore could not serve as a sufficient writing to satisfy the Statute of Frauds, Lee's act of filing the form constituted full performance of his oral promise. This mutual full execution of the oral agreement removes the Statute of Frauds as a bar to its enforcement.

Once an agreement to name a beneficiary is supported by consideration and fully executed, the designated beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest in the policy proceeds. Generally, an insured retains the right to change a beneficiary if the insurance policy or association by-laws permit it, meaning a standard beneficiary usually has a mere revocable expectancy. However, an insured can contract away the right to change the beneficiary. By entering into a binding contract and fully executing it by designating Dana, Lee contracted away his right to defeat Dana's interest. 

The children base their claim on the association's by-laws, which allowed Lee to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. While such by-laws govern the legal relationship between the fraternal association and the insured—and protect the association from competing claims—they do not destroy the vested equitable rights of a beneficiary arising from a valid contract. Because Dana held a vested equitable interest in the certificate, Lee no longer had the equitable right to change the beneficiary to his children. His later attempt to do so breached the executed contract and is legally ineffective against Dana's superior equitable claim. Therefore, Dana is entitled to the insurance proceeds.

clusters.representativeText:
The children have the better claim.

Lee’s premarital promise to Dana is the weak point in Dana’s case. A promise made “if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary” is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, that kind of agreement generally must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Here, no signed writing memorialized Lee’s promise. Dana’s marriage to Lee does not cure that defect; marriage is the very consideration that brings the agreement within the statute, and performance by marrying ordinarily does not take such an oral antenuptial agreement out of the statute.

So Dana cannot enforce the premarital oral promise as a binding contract requiring Lee to keep her as beneficiary.

Her second argument—that Lee actually did name her beneficiary after the marriage—is also not enough. In a fraternal insurance association, rights are governed by the certificate and the association’s by-laws. Those by-laws expressly allowed Lee to change beneficiaries later by filing a sworn request, and he did exactly that. Where the member retains the contractual right to change beneficiaries, the originally named beneficiary ordinarily has only an expectancy, not a vested property right, until the member dies without making a valid change.

Thus, when Lee first named Dana after the wedding, she acquired only a revocable expectancy under the certificate, because Lee still had the reserved power to substitute someone else. When he later complied with the association’s procedures and named his children, that change was effective, and the children became the proper beneficiaries at Lee’s death.

Dana could defeat the later change only if she could show an enforceable separate contract by which Lee bound himself not to change the beneficiary and to keep her as the recipient of the proceeds. But the only such contract she has is the oral premarital promise, and that is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

Accordingly, the children, as the later duly designated beneficiaries under the association’s rules, are entitled to the insurance proceeds.

Dana has the better claim to the insurance proceeds because she acquired a vested equitable interest in the certificate that prevents Lee from subsequently changing the beneficiary. 

The first legal issue involves the Statute of Frauds. Lee’s pre-marriage promise to name Dana as the beneficiary if she married him was an oral contract made in consideration of marriage. Under the Statute of Frauds, agreements made in consideration of marriage must be in writing to be enforceable. Furthermore, it is a well-settled rule that the act of marriage itself does not constitute sufficient part performance to remove an oral contract from the Statute of Frauds. Therefore, immediately after the wedding, the oral agreement remained unenforceable, and Dana could not have legally compelled Lee to name her as the beneficiary. 

However, the Statute of Frauds only applies to executory contracts, not to those that have been fully executed. Although Lee was not legally bound to fulfill his oral promise, he voluntarily performed his side of the agreement by filing the association’s standard form naming Dana as the beneficiary after the marriage. Because Dana had already performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee, Lee’s act of designating her as the beneficiary fully executed the oral contract. Once an oral contract is fully performed by both parties, it is removed from the Statute of Frauds and becomes legally binding. 

The next issue is whether Lee retained the right to change the beneficiary to his children under the association's by-laws. Generally, the beneficiary of a life insurance policy or a fraternal benefit certificate has a mere expectancy—not a vested right—if the insured retains the power to change the beneficiary. Furthermore, fraternal associations typically have strict by-laws governing these certificates, which courts enforce. 

However, equity recognizes a major exception to this rule. An insured can contract away the right to change a beneficiary. When an insured designates a beneficiary pursuant to a valid contract supported by valuable consideration—in this case, Dana's marriage to Lee—the beneficiary acquires a vested equitable interest in the policy proceeds. While the association’s by-laws permitting a change of beneficiary are valid and govern the relationship between the association and its members, they are designed for the protection of the association. They do not defeat the equitable rights established between competing claimants. 

Because Dana provided valuable consideration and the contract was fully executed, her equitable interest in the insurance proceeds vested. This vested interest stripped Lee of his equitable power to exercise the by-law provision to change the beneficiary to someone else. Lee’s subsequent act of naming his children as beneficiaries was a breach of his executed agreement with Dana. Since the children are merely gratuitous donees who provided no consideration for their designation, their claim is subordinate to Dana’s prior vested equitable interest. Therefore, Dana is entitled to the insurance proceeds.

The children have the better claim to the life insurance proceeds. The resolution of this dispute depends on whether Dana acquired a vested, irrevocable right to the policy based on Lee’s oral promise, or whether Lee retained the legal right to change the beneficiary to his children. 

Under the Statute of Frauds, any agreement made in consideration of marriage must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged to be legally enforceable. Lee’s promise to make Dana the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate was made expressly in consideration of her marrying him. Because this agreement was entirely oral and Lee never signed a written agreement memorializing the promise, the contract falls squarely within the Statute of Frauds and is generally unenforceable.

Dana cannot overcome the Statute of Frauds by arguing that her act of marrying Lee constituted "part performance." It is a well-established legal rule that marriage alone does not constitute sufficient part performance to take an oral prenuptial contract out of the Statute of Frauds. If marriage were enough to bypass the requirement, the specific provision of the Statute of Frauds governing agreements in consideration of marriage would be rendered entirely meaningless. 

Furthermore, Lee’s act of filing the association’s standard form naming Dana as his beneficiary after the wedding does not satisfy the writing requirement of the Statute of Frauds. To satisfy the Statute, a writing must contain the essential terms of the contract. A standard change-of-beneficiary form merely directs the insurer on whom to pay upon the insured's death. It does not reference the oral prenuptial agreement, state that the designation is irrevocable, or indicate that the designation was made in consideration of marriage. Therefore, the standard form does not serve as a sufficient written memorandum of the oral contract.

Because the oral agreement is legally unenforceable, Dana did not acquire a vested equitable or legal interest in the life insurance certificate. In the context of life insurance—and particularly within fraternal benefit associations where the by-laws explicitly grant the member the right to change the beneficiary—a named beneficiary possesses only a mere expectancy, not a vested property right. A beneficiary's interest only vests irrevocably during the insured's lifetime if there is a valid, enforceable contract preventing the insured from altering the designation. 

Absent an enforceable contract binding him to keep Dana as the beneficiary, Lee retained full authority under the association’s by-laws to change his designation at any time. Lee strictly followed the association's authorized procedure by filing a sworn request to name his two children from his earlier marriage as the new beneficiaries. Because this subsequent change was procedurally valid and not barred by any enforceable legal restriction, the children's rights to the proceeds vested upon Lee's death. Consequently, the children have the superior claim to the insurance proceeds.

# Analysis of Dana's Claim vs. the Children's Claim to Insurance Proceeds

This question requires analysis of several intersecting doctrines: the enforceability of Lee's oral pre-marriage promise, the effect of the beneficiary designation and subsequent change under the fraternal association's by-laws, and the extent to which Dana can assert an equitable or contractual right that overrides the association's standard procedures.

## Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Lee orally promised Dana before their marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary of his life-insurance certificate. Dana's act of marrying Lee constitutes consideration for that promise—marriage is classic and well-recognized consideration for a contract. The question is whether this oral promise is enforceable despite the absence of a written agreement.

Under the Statute of Frauds, contracts made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) generally must be in writing to be enforceable. Lee's promise—"if you marry me, I will make you my beneficiary"—is precisely the type of agreement the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds was designed to cover. Because no written agreement memorializing this promise was ever signed by Lee, the promise is unenforceable at law under the Statute of Frauds.

However, Dana might argue that the Statute of Frauds is satisfied by part performance or should be overcome by equitable estoppel. Dana fully performed her side of the bargain by marrying Lee, and Lee himself partially performed by actually filing the beneficiary designation naming Dana. Courts in some jurisdictions have held that where the promisee has fully performed and the promisor has partially performed, the Statute of Frauds does not bar enforcement. The doctrine of equitable estoppel or part performance could arguably remove the oral promise from the Statute's bar, particularly because Dana changed her position irrevocably by entering into the marriage.

## The Beneficiary Designation and the Power to Change

Even assuming Dana can overcome the Statute of Frauds problem, she faces a second significant obstacle: the fraternal association's by-laws expressly permitted Lee to change his beneficiary designation at any time by filing a sworn request. Lee followed this procedure and named his two children as the new beneficiaries. Under standard insurance law principles, a policyholder who retains the right to change beneficiaries may do so freely, and the named beneficiary at the time of death is entitled to the proceeds. A beneficiary designation is generally revocable unless the beneficiary has a **vested** right that cannot be unilaterally altered.

Under the majority rule, a named beneficiary in a life-insurance policy where the insured retains the right to change beneficiaries has a **mere expectancy**, not a vested right. The beneficiary's interest does not vest until the insured's death, and the insured may change the designation at will. Under this principle, Lee's later designation of his children would control, and Dana would have no claim based solely on the earlier designation.

## Dana's Theory: Irrevocable Beneficiary Designation Through Contract

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise, supported by the consideration of marriage, created a **contract** that effectively made her designation **irrevocable**—that is, Lee's promise not only obligated him to name her as beneficiary but also implicitly obligated him not to change that designation. Under this theory, even though the association's by-laws permitted changes, Lee had contractually bound himself to Dana not to exercise that right. Dana would seek to impose a **constructive trust** on the proceeds in the hands of the children, arguing that Lee breached his contractual obligation and that the children, as volunteers (they gave no consideration for being named), should not be permitted to benefit from that breach.

Courts have recognized this theory in analogous cases. Where an insured makes an enforceable agreement—typically in a divorce settlement or separation agreement—to maintain a particular beneficiary designation, and then breaches that agreement by changing the beneficiary, courts have imposed constructive trusts on the proceeds in favor of the intended beneficiary. The key requirements are: (1) an enforceable agreement supported by consideration, (2) breach of that agreement by changing the beneficiary, and (3) the new beneficiaries are volunteers rather than bona fide purchasers for value.

Here, the children gave no consideration for being named as beneficiaries. If Dana can establish an enforceable contract, equity would impose a constructive trust on the proceeds for her benefit, because the children's claim rests solely on a designation that Lee had contractually promised not to make.

## The Critical Question: Enforceability of the Oral Promise

The entire analysis turns on whether Dana can enforce the oral promise despite the Statute of Frauds. If she cannot, the children prevail straightforwardly under the last-filed beneficiary designation. If she can, she has a strong equitable claim to a constructive trust.

**Arguments favoring enforceability (Dana's position):**

1. **Full performance by the promisee.** Dana fully performed by marrying Lee. Many courts hold that full performance by one party takes an oral contract out of the Statute of Frauds, or at minimum triggers equitable estoppel against the promisor (or those claiming through the promisor) who would use the Statute as a shield for fraud.

2. **Part performance by the promisor.** Lee himself performed the promise by filing the beneficiary designation. This corroborates the existence and terms of the oral agreement and reduces concerns about fraudulent claims.

3. **Irreversible change of position.** Marriage is an irrevocable act—Dana cannot be restored to her pre-marriage status. Allowing the Statute of Frauds to defeat her claim would work a fraud upon her, which is precisely what equity seeks to prevent.

4. **Equitable estoppel.** The children are not parties to the insurance contract and gave no consideration. Allowing them to invoke the Statute of Frauds (a defense personal to the promisor) to defeat Dana's equitable claim may be impermissible.

**Arguments against enforceability (the children's position):**

1. **The Statute of Frauds is clear.** Agreements made in consideration of marriage must be in writing. The legislature enacted this requirement precisely because of the high stakes and emotional context surrounding marriage, making oral promises particularly unreliable.

2. **The part-performance doctrine varies by jurisdiction.** Not all jurisdictions recognize part performance as removing an oral agreement from the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds (as opposed to the real-property provision, where part performance is more widely accepted).

3. **Policy favoring free alienability of beneficiary designations.** Insurance law strongly favors the insured's right to change beneficiaries. Allowing oral promises to override formal beneficiary changes would create uncertainty and invite fraudulent claims by alleged promisees.

4. **The children stand in Lee's shoes for purposes of the by-laws.** The association's by-laws authorized the change, Lee followed proper procedures, and the children's rights derive from the association's contract with Lee, not from any wrongful act.

## The Better Claim

**The children likely have the better claim to the insurance proceeds**, though the question is close and jurisdiction-dependent.

The majority of courts addressing this precise scenario have held that an **oral** antenuptial promise to maintain a beneficiary designation is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds' marriage provision. While courts are sympathetic to the equitable arguments Dana can raise, the Statute of Frauds serves an important evidentiary function, and courts are reluctant to enforce oral promises—particularly after the promisor is dead and cannot testify about the terms or existence of the agreement. The parol evidence concerns are especially acute here because the only surviving party with knowledge of the alleged promise is the very person who stands to benefit from its enforcement.

Moreover, from the perspective of the fraternal association, the by-laws created a clear procedure for changing beneficiaries, and Lee followed it. The association is entitled to pay the proceeds to the last-named beneficiary of record, and courts generally uphold the regularity of such designations to preserve the administrability of insurance contracts.

That said, if Dana were in a jurisdiction that broadly applies equitable estoppel or the part-performance doctrine to the marriage provision of the Statute of Frauds, she could prevail. Her argument is strongest if the court views the Statute of Frauds as a rule designed to prevent fraud and concludes that enforcing it here would *perpetrate* a fraud—Lee induced Dana into marriage with a promise he later repudiated, and his estate (through his children) should not benefit from that broken promise.

**In summary:** Under the prevailing legal rules, the children have the better claim because (1) the oral promise is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, (2) the by-laws authorized the beneficiary change, (3) Lee followed proper procedures, and (4) the last-filed designation controls. Dana's equitable arguments—constructive trust, estoppel, and part performance—are meritorious but face the substantial barrier of the Statute of Frauds and the strong policy favoring the insured's freedom to change beneficiaries. Were the promise in writing, Dana would almost certainly prevail through a constructive trust theory, since the children are mere volunteers. The absence of a writing is dispositive in most jurisdictions.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem raises intersecting issues of contract law, insurance law, and the enforceability of promises regarding beneficiary designations. The central question is whether Dana can enforce Lee's pre-marriage oral promise to make her the beneficiary, or whether the children's claim under the later, formally executed beneficiary change prevails.

## Dana's Potential Theories

**1. Breach of Contract Based on the Oral Promise**

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—was a contract supported by valid consideration. Marriage is classic and well-recognized consideration for a promise. Dana performed by marrying Lee, and Lee initially performed by filing the beneficiary designation naming her. Dana would argue that the promise implicitly included an obligation not to later change the beneficiary, or at least that the promise created a vested contractual right in her that Lee could not unilaterally revoke.

However, this theory faces several significant obstacles:

*Statute of Frauds.* In many jurisdictions, a promise made in consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. The problem states that no written agreement memorializing the promise was ever signed by Lee. Under the traditional Statute of Frauds provision covering promises made in consideration of marriage (distinct from the mutual promises to marry, which are excluded), Dana's claim based on the oral promise would be unenforceable unless an exception applies.

Dana might argue that the Statute of Frauds is satisfied by part performance or estoppel. She did marry Lee (full performance of her side of the bargain), and Lee did initially designate her as beneficiary. Some courts hold that full performance by one party takes an oral contract outside the Statute of Frauds, reasoning that the equities demand enforcement when the promisee has fully performed and cannot be restored to the status quo ante. Marriage, once performed, obviously cannot be "undone" as a practical matter. This is a plausible argument, but courts are not uniform on whether the marriage-provision exception can be overcome by performance alone, and some jurisdictions strictly enforce the writing requirement.

*Parol Evidence and Interpretation.* Even if the oral promise is enforceable despite the Statute of Frauds, there is a question about its scope. Did Lee promise to make Dana the beneficiary permanently and irrevocably, or merely to designate her at that time? The promise as stated—"he would make her the beneficiary"—is arguably ambiguous. Lee did make her the beneficiary. He could contend (were he alive) that the promise was fully performed when the designation was filed, and that it did not include a promise never to change the designation. Dana would need to establish that the promise encompassed an irrevocable commitment.

**2. Constructive Trust or Equitable Estoppel**

Even if the oral contract is unenforceable at law, Dana might seek equitable relief. Courts have sometimes imposed constructive trusts on insurance proceeds where a beneficiary change was procured in violation of a contractual obligation, on the theory that allowing the new beneficiaries to take the proceeds would unjustly enrich them. Under this theory, the children would hold the proceeds as constructive trustees for Dana.

However, constructive trust is an equitable remedy typically applied when there has been fraud, breach of a fiduciary duty, or unjust enrichment traceable to wrongful conduct. The children are innocent third parties—Lee's own children from a prior marriage—and they did nothing wrongful. The question is whether Lee's breach of his alleged promise to Dana is sufficient wrongful conduct to warrant the imposition of a constructive trust. Courts have reached different conclusions, but many require a clear and enforceable underlying obligation before imposing such a trust.

## The Children's Claim

**1. The Association's By-Laws Control**

The children's strongest argument is grounded in insurance law principles. The insurance certificate was issued by a fraternal benefit association, and the association's by-laws expressly permitted members to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed the prescribed procedure exactly. Under well-established insurance law, the insured member retains the right to change beneficiaries unless that right has been contractually surrendered in a manner recognized by the insurance contract or by-laws. A named beneficiary under a policy that reserves the right to change beneficiaries has a mere expectancy, not a vested right.

The by-laws here allowed changes, and Lee exercised that right through proper channels. The association is obligated to pay according to the last valid beneficiary designation on file, which names the children.

**2. The Oral Promise Does Not Bind the Association or Override the By-Laws**

The fraternal association was not a party to Lee's oral promise to Dana. Insurance associations pay according to their governing documents. Even if Lee breached a private agreement with Dana, that breach does not invalidate the beneficiary change as between Lee and the association. The association's records show the children as the designated beneficiaries, and the association would be entitled—indeed obligated—to pay accordingly.

**3. Statute of Frauds Defense**

The children, as third-party beneficiaries of the insurance proceeds, can raise the Statute of Frauds as a defense to Dana's contract claim. Since no writing memorializes Lee's promise, and the promise was made in consideration of marriage, the children would argue the promise is unenforceable.

**4. No Consideration Running to the Children / Innocent Takers**

The children gave no consideration and made no promise; they are simply the last-named beneficiaries under a validly executed change. They stand on the formal legal documentation.

## The Likely Outcome

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds, for several reinforcing reasons:

First, Lee's oral promise to Dana is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as a promise made in consideration of marriage that was never reduced to a signed writing. While Dana's full performance (the marriage) creates an argument for taking the promise outside the statute, this argument is uncertain and jurisdiction-dependent, and many courts would decline to enforce the oral promise.

Second, even if the oral promise were somehow enforceable, its terms are ambiguous as to whether Lee promised permanent, irrevocable beneficiary status or merely promised to designate Dana at the time. The more natural reading is that Lee promised to name her as beneficiary—which he did—not that he promised never to exercise his contractual right under the by-laws to make a change.

Third, and most fundamentally, insurance law strongly favors honoring the last properly executed beneficiary designation. The association's by-laws expressly reserved to Lee the right to change beneficiaries, and he exercised that right through the prescribed procedure. A beneficiary under a revocable designation has no vested interest in the policy proceeds—only an expectancy that can be defeated at any time. Dana's designation was revocable, and it was revoked.

Fourth, even under an equitable theory, courts are generally reluctant to impose constructive trusts that override formal insurance beneficiary designations absent clear evidence of fraud, undue influence, or a written agreement restricting the right to change beneficiaries. An unwritten, arguably ambiguous oral promise—even one supported by the consideration of marriage—is typically insufficient.

Dana's remedy, if any, would lie in a breach of contract action against Lee's estate (assuming the Statute of Frauds could be overcome), not in a direct claim to the insurance proceeds themselves. But even that claim is weakened by the Statute of Frauds problem and the ambiguity of the promise. The insurance proceeds themselves should be paid to the children as the last validly designated beneficiaries under the association's governing documents.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem presents a conflict between Dana, who claims the proceeds based on (1) Lee's oral pre-marriage promise and (2) his initial post-marriage beneficiary designation, and Lee's two children, who claim under the later beneficiary change executed in compliance with the association's by-laws. The children have the better claim, though the analysis requires careful consideration of several doctrines.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's strongest theoretical argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—supported by the consideration of her agreement to marry him, created a binding contract that should be specifically enforced or that should estop Lee (or his children as successors) from changing the beneficiary designation.

**Consideration.** Marriage is valid consideration for a promise. Dana's agreement to marry Lee in exchange for the beneficiary designation would satisfy the consideration requirement of contract formation. Courts have long recognized that marriage constitutes sufficient consideration to support a contractual obligation, distinguishing such promises from gratuitous undertakings.

**Statute of Frauds.** However, Dana faces a formidable obstacle. Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, a promise made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was entirely oral, and no written agreement memorializing it was ever signed by Lee. The facts are explicit on this point. Dana would need to overcome the Statute of Frauds to enforce the oral promise.

Dana might argue part performance or estoppel to avoid the Statute of Frauds. She did, after all, marry Lee in reliance on the promise, and Lee did initially perform by naming her as beneficiary. Some jurisdictions recognize that where a party has fully performed under an oral agreement (here, Dana performed by marrying Lee), the Statute of Frauds should not bar enforcement. However, the weight of authority on promises in consideration of marriage tends to enforce the writing requirement strictly, particularly because the marriage itself is not unambiguously referable to the insurance promise—people marry for many reasons, and the act of marrying does not, standing alone, point unmistakably to the existence of an agreement regarding insurance proceeds. Courts applying the "unequivocal referability" test for part performance would likely find Dana's marriage insufficient to take the oral promise outside the statute.

**Even if enforceable, the promise may have been fulfilled.** Lee promised to "make her the beneficiary," and he did exactly that after the marriage by filing the standard form. One could argue the promise was fully performed at that point. The promise, by its terms, was to "make" Dana the beneficiary—not to keep her as beneficiary irrevocably and permanently. This is a plausible interpretation that would further undermine Dana's claim.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Initial Beneficiary Designation

Dana might argue that because Lee named her as beneficiary after the wedding, and because that designation was supported by the consideration of marriage, the designation became irrevocable—that is, it vested in Dana a contractual right that Lee could not unilaterally revoke.

**The general rule on beneficiary changes.** In fraternal benefit association insurance, the prevailing rule is that the member retains the right to change beneficiaries in accordance with the association's by-laws, and the named beneficiary has no vested interest in the certificate during the member's lifetime. The beneficiary's interest is a mere expectancy. The association's by-laws here expressly permitted the member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request, and Lee followed that procedure. Under standard insurance law principles, the later designation of the children would control.

**The exception for beneficiaries supported by consideration.** The critical exception to the general rule is that where a beneficiary designation is supported by a separate, valid, enforceable contract—where the insured has promised for valuable consideration to designate and maintain a particular person as beneficiary—the designation may become irrevocable despite by-law provisions permitting changes. This is sometimes described as creating an equitable interest in the beneficiary that equity will protect.

For this exception to apply, Dana must establish the existence of an enforceable contract. This brings us back to the Statute of Frauds problem. The oral promise, made in consideration of marriage, is unenforceable absent a writing under the Statute of Frauds. Without an enforceable underlying contract, the beneficiary designation carries no greater weight than any revocable designation, and Lee was free to change it.

Some courts have held that where the insured actually designated the promisee as beneficiary, the designation itself constitutes sufficient written evidence or part performance to satisfy the Statute of Frauds, effectively merging the oral promise with the written designation form. This is a minority approach and is analytically questionable, because the beneficiary designation form is not a memorandum of the marriage-consideration agreement—it is simply the standard insurance form and does not reference the underlying bargain or the consideration of marriage. It is not signed as a contract between Lee and Dana; it is a directive to the association.

## The Children's Claim

The children's position rests on straightforward principles of insurance law. Lee had the right under the association's by-laws to change his beneficiary designation. He exercised that right by filing a sworn request naming his two children. The change was procedurally proper. The last valid beneficiary designation controls the distribution of proceeds upon the insured's death. The children are therefore the properly designated beneficiaries at the time of Lee's death.

The children would further argue that even if Lee's oral promise to Dana were otherwise enforceable, it was barred by the Statute of Frauds; that the promise, by its terms, was to "make" Dana the beneficiary, which Lee did, and that the promise did not include an obligation to maintain her as beneficiary permanently; and that the beneficiary designation form filed with the association does not constitute a written memorandum of the alleged contract sufficient to satisfy the Statute of Frauds.

## Equitable Arguments

Dana might seek relief in equity, arguing detrimental reliance or unjust enrichment. She married Lee in reliance on his promise and is now being deprived of the promised benefit. However, equity's power to impose a constructive trust on insurance proceeds or to override a valid beneficiary change is generally limited to cases involving fraud, duress, undue influence, or a clear enforceable contract. Dana received the benefit of the marriage itself—whatever that entailed—and courts are generally reluctant to use equitable doctrines to override clear by-law provisions and properly executed beneficiary changes, especially where the underlying promise fails the Statute of Frauds.

If Dana could establish fraud—for example, that Lee married her solely to induce the marriage with no intention of maintaining the designation—she might have a tort or equitable claim. But the facts do not suggest fraud; Lee did designate her initially and changed the designation only "several years afterward," suggesting changed circumstances rather than fraudulent intent.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral promise to Dana, though supported by the consideration of marriage, is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was never reduced to a writing signed by Lee. Without an enforceable contract, the beneficiary designation in Dana's favor was revocable under the association's by-laws. Lee properly exercised his right to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request naming his children, and the last valid designation controls. Dana's equitable arguments, while sympathetic, are insufficient to overcome the Statute of Frauds bar and the clear operation of the association's by-laws permitting beneficiary changes. The proceeds should be paid to Lee's two children as the last-named beneficiaries.

# Analysis of the Competing Claims to Lee's Insurance Proceeds

This problem presents a conflict between Dana, who claims the proceeds based on (1) Lee's oral pre-marriage promise and (2) his initial post-marriage designation of her as beneficiary, and Lee's two children from a prior marriage, who claim under Lee's later beneficiary change executed in accordance with the association's by-laws. The children have the better claim, though Dana may have a separate cause of action for breach of contract. The analysis requires examination of several intersecting doctrines.

## The Nature of Fraternal Benefit Certificates and the Right to Change Beneficiaries

A fraternal insurance certificate is not an ordinary commercial life-insurance policy; it is a contract between the member and the association, governed by the association's charter and by-laws, which are incorporated into the certificate by reference. The by-laws here expressly permitted a member to change beneficiaries by filing a sworn request. Lee followed that procedure when he designated his children. Under the general rule, where the insured retains the power to change the beneficiary—and the certificate or by-laws grant that power—the named beneficiary has a mere expectancy, not a vested right, in the proceeds. The beneficiary's interest does not vest until the insured's death, and the insured may freely exercise the power of revocation at any time during life, provided the prescribed procedure is followed. Because Lee complied with the association's by-laws when he redesignated his children, the change was effective on its face.

## Dana's Claim Based on Lee's Oral Pre-Marriage Promise

Dana's strongest argument is that Lee's oral promise—"if you marry me, I will make you the beneficiary"—supported by the consideration of marriage, created a binding contract that should be specifically enforced against the proceeds, effectively estopping Lee (and now his estate and the children) from changing the beneficiary designation.

**The Statute of Frauds Problem.** In most jurisdictions, a promise made in consideration of marriage falls within the Statute of Frauds and must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Lee's promise was entirely oral, and no written memorandum was ever signed. Dana will argue that the marriage itself constitutes part performance sufficient to take the agreement outside the Statute. Courts have been divided on this point. Some courts hold that marriage alone is not sufficient part performance because it does not unequivocally refer to the terms of the alleged contract (unlike, say, taking possession of land in a real-estate context). Other courts, particularly in equity, have been more willing to find that marriage plus additional acts of reliance may satisfy the part-performance exception. Here, however, the only "performance" Dana can point to is the marriage itself and Lee's subsequent (voluntary) designation of her—which Lee later revoked. The weight of authority would likely hold that the oral promise is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds because it was a promise made in consideration of marriage without a signed writing.

**Even if the Statute of Frauds Is Overcome.** Assuming Dana can get past the Statute of Frauds—perhaps by invoking estoppel or a jurisdiction that treats marriage as sufficient part performance—she must still show that the oral agreement created an irrevocable designation. The difficulty is that the fraternal association's by-laws, which form part of the insurance contract, expressly reserved to the member the right to change beneficiaries. A separate side agreement between the insured and the beneficiary can, in some circumstances, override this power if it amounts to a valid contract supported by consideration to maintain the beneficiary designation. Courts have recognized that where an insured, for valuable consideration, agrees not to change the beneficiary, equity may impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in favor of the promisee even after a formal change has been made. Marriage is generally regarded as valid consideration. So if the oral promise were enforceable, Dana would have a colorable equitable claim.

However, the critical issue is that the promise must be proven to have been a binding commitment to irrevocably designate Dana—not merely a promise to name her, which Lee fulfilled and then later changed. Lee promised to "make her the beneficiary," which he did. One could argue that the promise was fully performed when he filed the form naming Dana, and that the promise did not include an implicit covenant never to change the designation thereafter. Under this reading, Lee kept his promise; the subsequent change was within his contractual rights under the certificate. Dana would need to prove that the promise implicitly included a commitment to maintain the designation permanently, which is a difficult evidentiary burden, especially given the oral and informal nature of the promise.

## Dana's Claim Based on the Initial Beneficiary Designation

Dana might argue that once Lee designated her as beneficiary after the marriage, her status became irrevocable because it was supported by the consideration of marriage. This argument conflates two distinct things: the side agreement between Lee and Dana, and the beneficiary designation under the insurance certificate. The designation itself is governed by the certificate and the association's by-laws, which expressly permitted changes. The designation, standing alone, confers only an expectancy. It does not become irrevocable merely because the beneficiary also happens to have given consideration for a separate promise. The irrevocability, if any, must flow from the separate contract, not from the designation form itself. And as discussed above, the separate contract (the oral promise) faces the Statute of Frauds barrier and interpretive difficulties.

## The Children's Claim

The children's claim is straightforward. Lee executed a change of beneficiary in strict compliance with the association's by-laws. At the time of Lee's death, they were the designated beneficiaries of record. Under the general rule, the association is obligated to pay the proceeds to the last properly designated beneficiary. The children's designation was the last valid one. Unless Dana can establish a superior equitable claim—which, for the reasons discussed, is unlikely—the children are entitled to the proceeds.

Courts have consistently held that fraternal associations must pay the beneficiary designated in accordance with their by-laws, and that disputes between claimants based on side agreements do not affect the association's obligation to pay the record beneficiary. The association can interplead the funds if there is a genuine dispute, but the substantive question of entitlement favors the children.

## Equitable Remedies and Constructive Trust

Dana's best remaining argument is for a constructive trust. Even where a beneficiary change is formally valid, courts in equity have imposed constructive trusts on insurance proceeds where the change was procured by fraud, undue influence, or in violation of a binding contractual obligation. If Dana could prove (1) that the oral promise was enforceable despite the Statute of Frauds, (2) that the promise included an implicit or explicit commitment never to change the designation, and (3) that Lee's subsequent change violated that commitment, a court might impose a constructive trust on the proceeds in Dana's favor.

But each of these elements presents significant obstacles. The Statute of Frauds is the most formidable. Additionally, there is no indication of fraud or undue influence in Lee's decision to change beneficiaries—he simply exercised a right reserved to him under the by-laws. Courts are reluctant to impose constructive trusts absent clear and convincing evidence of a binding obligation, and the informality and ambiguity of Lee's oral promise undercut Dana's case.

## Dana's Possible Breach-of-Contract Action

It is worth noting that even if Dana cannot claim the insurance proceeds directly, she may have a breach-of-contract action against Lee's estate—assuming she can overcome the Statute of Frauds. If the oral promise is enforceable, Lee's change of beneficiary would constitute a breach, and Dana could recover damages (potentially measured by the value of the insurance proceeds) from the estate. This is a personal claim against the estate, however, not a claim to the insurance proceeds themselves, and it would be subject to the estate's solvency and the priority of other claims.

## Conclusion

The children have the better claim to the insurance proceeds. Lee's oral pre-marriage promise to Dana is likely unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as a promise made in consideration of marriage without a signed writing. Even if enforceable, the promise is ambiguous as to whether it included a commitment never to change the designation. Lee complied with the association's by-laws when he redesignated his children, and the last valid beneficiary designation controls. Dana's equitable arguments for a constructive trust face substantial evidentiary and legal hurdles. While Dana may have a separate action against Lee's estate for breach of the oral promise (if she can establish its enforceability), the proceeds themselves should be paid to the children as the last properly designated beneficiaries.
